proposals.title: Evaluating the Necessity of Specialist Hubs: A Multi-Agent Simulation of Peer-Only Networks for Complex Disease Management
proposals.motivation: Task-shifting to non-specialist community health workers (CHWs), like India's ASHAs, is a crucial strategy to address the global mental health treatment gap. However, for complex, chronic diseases, the optimal balance between decentralized peer-led care and centralized specialist supervision remains unclear. This research addresses a core challenge in scalable healthcare delivery: determining when specialist-led hubs are essential for quality versus when peer networks alone can be effective.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for complex disease management, peer-only primary care networks will exhibit a performance plateau or degradation in patient outcomes beyond a certain disease complexity threshold, and that the integration of a specialist hub for consultation and periodic fine-tuning of care protocols is necessary to maintain quality.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent simulation framework where patient agents with varying disease complexity profiles are managed by networks of CHW agents. We will model two architectures: 1) a decentralized peer-only network with local communication (analogous to Local SGD), and 2) a hub-and-spoke model with a specialist agent that periodically aggregates case data and fine-tunes the decision parameters of the CHW agents (analogous to federated learning with a central server). The CHW agents' decision-making will be modeled using lightweight transformer-based policies, pre-trained on clinical guidelines and fine-tuned via reinforcement learning on simulated patient trajectories. The key metric is the long-term patient outcome score as a function of disease complexity and network topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, computational framework to model care delivery architectures, offering evidence-based guidance for health system design. It will contribute to the machine learning community by adapting concepts from distributed optimization (e.g., Local SGD generalization) and model tuning to a novel, socially impactful domain. The findings will directly inform policy on the design of task-shifted care networks for non-communicable and psychiatric diseases.
proposals.novelty: While task-shifting is well-studied, applying multi-agent simulation and distributed training paradigms from ML to rigorously model the *structural necessity* of specialist hubs in care networks is novel. The approach moves beyond observational studies to perform controlled experiments on system architecture, a method not commonly applied in health services research pre-2026.

proposals.title: Evaluating Asynchronous Learning Modalities for Scaling Mental Health Provider Training: A Randomized Controlled Trial in Low-Resource Settings
proposals.motivation: The global treatment gap for mental health disorders is exacerbated by a shortage of trained providers, particularly in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). While telemedicine, especially live video-conferencing, has shown promise for provider training, its synchronous nature creates logistical and cost barriers to scaling. Asynchronous tools like store-and-forward platforms and AI-driven chatbots offer a potentially scalable alternative, but their efficacy relative to synchronous video for complex clinical skill acquisition remains unproven in rigorous trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid training protocol, combining an initial synchronous video session with subsequent asynchronous reinforcement via a fine-tuned large language model (LLM) chatbot and store-and-forward case reviews, will achieve non-inferior clinical competency outcomes compared to a fully synchronous video-based training protocol for Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs) delivering a brief psychological intervention.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-arm, parallel-group, randomized controlled trial in South India. ASHAs will be randomized to: 1) Standard synchronous video-conferencing training, 2) Asynchronous training using a store-and-forward platform with expert feedback, and 3) A hybrid arm with initial video followed by asynchronous practice with a chatbot fine-tuned on therapeutic dialogue. The primary outcome is competency score on a validated role-play assessment. We will employ causal inference methods and analyze the generalization dynamics of the training protocols, drawing on principles from distributed optimization to understand how different communication frequencies (synchronous vs. asynchronous updates) affect the convergence and generalization of learned clinical skills.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal evidence on the comparative effectiveness of synchronous versus asynchronous telemedicine modalities for training non-specialist health workers in mental health care. It will yield a practical, scalable training framework for LMICs and contribute to the theoretical understanding of how learning dynamics in humans mirror phenomena in distributed machine learning, such as the generalization benefits of local steps (asynchronous periods) between synchronizations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied telemedicine for direct patient care or one-off training, this proposal uniquely frames provider skill acquisition as a distributed optimization problem and rigorously tests whether strategically introduced asynchrony can maintain efficacy while drastically improving scalability, a non-obvious claim given the presumed necessity of live interaction for complex skill transfer.

proposals.title: Optimizing Treatment Protocols via Local Fine-Tuning: A Machine Learning Framework for Community-Specific HCV Care
proposals.motivation: The global scale-up of direct-acting antiviral (DAA) therapy for Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) faces a critical challenge: a one-size-fits-all treatment protocol may not be optimal across diverse socio-cultural and healthcare delivery contexts, potentially impacting patient adherence and outcomes. This problem sits at the intersection of algorithmic fairness in healthcare and the need for personalized, scalable public health interventions, a key area of interest in machine learning for global health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a treatment protocol framework that allows for localized, community-specific adaptation—informed by local patient demographics, health system factors, and social determinants—will lead to statistically significant improvements in treatment adherence and sustained virologic response (SVR) rates compared to a rigid, uniform protocol.
proposals.method: We will frame protocol adaptation as a constrained optimization problem. Using data from a multi-site HCV treatment program (e.g., involving Accredited Social Health Activists in South India), we will treat each site as a distinct 'task'. A base treatment protocol (e.g., a decision policy for follow-up scheduling, counseling intensity, and side-effect management) will be represented as a parameterized model. We will then apply a meta-learning or federated learning approach, akin to personalized fine-tuning in NLP, where the base model is adapted to each site using local patient data. The core algorithmic comparison will be between a centrally 'trained' uniform model (SGD) and locally adapted models (Local SGD/FedAvg), evaluating their generalization performance on site-specific adherence and outcome metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel machine learning framework for the data-driven design of adaptive public health protocols. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between standardization and localization in treatment delivery. Furthermore, it will extend theoretical insights from distributed optimization (like Local SGD generalization) to a high-stakes, real-world domain, demonstrating how algorithmic personalization can bridge treatment gaps in psychiatric and infectious disease care.
proposals.novelty: While protocol adaptation is discussed in public health, formalizing it as a machine learning optimization problem with explicit generalization bounds is novel. The approach non-trivially connects Arora's work on why Local SGD generalizes better to a healthcare context, testing if local updates (site adaptations) yield better 'generalization' (patient outcomes) than a single global update (uniform protocol).

proposals.title: Adaptive Tuning of the ECHO Model for Diverse Underserved Settings: A Multi-Site Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: The Project ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes) model has demonstrated efficacy in improving care for specific conditions in rural and prison settings by creating knowledge-sharing networks. However, its scalability and effectiveness across a broader spectrum of underserved populations—such as urban homeless communities, migrant worker populations, and indigenous groups—remain inadequately studied. Expanding this evidence base is critical to reducing the global treatment gap for mental health and chronic diseases in a wider array of resource-constrained environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematically adapted and fine-tuned ECHO implementation protocol, which incorporates local contextual factors and community health worker (ASHAs) feedback, will lead to significantly greater improvements in provider self-efficacy and patient outcomes in new, diverse underserved settings compared to a standardized, non-adapted ECHO deployment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, prospective cohort study implementing ECHO hubs for psychiatric and primary care in three distinct underserved settings: urban homeless shelters, agricultural migrant camps, and remote indigenous communities. The core method involves a two-phase 'ECHO-tuning' process: 1) A pre-implementation phase using participatory design with local Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs) and community leaders to adapt curriculum and delivery, and 2) An iterative tuning phase where participant engagement data and weekly self-efficacy scores are used to dynamically adjust session content and mentorship focus, applying principles from online learning and model fine-tuning. Primary outcomes will be measured via validated scales for provider confidence and retrospective analysis of de-identified patient health metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, replicable framework for the contextual adaptation of tele-mentoring networks, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all model. It will generate comparative effectiveness data on ECHO across diverse populations, offering actionable insights for global health implementers. The study also aims to formalize the 'tuning' process for community health interventions, drawing an analog to machine learning fine-tuning for better generalization.
proposals.novelty: While ECHO expansion is ongoing, this proposal novelly frames implementation as an 'adaptive tuning' problem, explicitly leveraging concepts from algorithmic training and participant feedback loops to optimize the intervention for new 'distributions' of underserved settings, rather than merely replicating a fixed model.

proposals.title: Decomposing the ECHO Effect: A Causal Framework for Identifying Critical Curriculum Components in Primary Care Provider Training
proposals.motivation: The Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes (ECHO) model is a proven tele-mentoring framework for expanding specialty care capacity among primary care providers, particularly for complex conditions like psychiatric disorders. However, ECHO programs are resource-intensive, combining didactic teaching, case presentations, and community building. To scale efficiently and reduce the global treatment gap, we must identify which specific training components are causally responsible for improvements in provider competency, rather than relying on the bundled intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the case-based learning and iterative feedback component of ECHO is the primary driver of measurable gains in provider self-efficacy and clinical knowledge, while didactic elements alone provide negligible long-term competency improvements.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-arm, longitudinal study randomizing primary care providers to different ECHO curriculum variants (e.g., full ECHO, didactic-only, case-discussion-only). We will treat provider competency outcomes (assessed via validated self-efficacy scales and knowledge tests) as time-series data. Inspired by analysis techniques for understanding generalization in iterative training algorithms like Local SGD, we will apply causal mediation analysis and gradient-based feature attribution methods to the longitudinal outcome data. This will allow us to decompose the total 'training effect' and attribute gains to specific curriculum 'modules' and their interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, component-level analysis of the ECHO training protocol, moving beyond outcome studies to mechanistic understanding. It will offer evidence-based guidelines for designing streamlined, maximally effective ECHO curricula, directly informing health policy and implementation science for scaling mental health care access.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying rigorous machine learning-inspired training analysis (e.g., gradient attribution, ablation studies) to a complex human learning system in healthcare, creating a novel bridge between optimization theory and implementation science to de-black-box a successful intervention.

proposals.title: Cascading Knowledge Transfer: A Train-the-Trainer Framework for Scaling Medical AI Model Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Project ECHO has demonstrated success in disseminating specialized medical knowledge to primary care providers via tele-mentoring, but scaling it requires significant expert time. Concurrently, fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for specialized domains like clinical decision support is data- and compute-intensive. A method to efficiently propagate expert knowledge through a network of models, akin to training community health workers, could address both bottlenecks.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical, train-the-trainer fine-tuning paradigm, inspired by public health dissemination models, can enable a central expert model to effectively train a cascade of intermediate 'trainer' models, which in turn can fine-tune downstream 'practitioner' models for specific tasks with minimal loss in performance compared to direct expert fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Local SGD framework, where models are trained independently on local data and periodically averaged, to a structured hierarchy. A centrally fine-tuned expert LLM (e.g., a clinical BERT or GPT variant) will be used to generate synthetic training data and fine-tune a first tier of 'trainer' models on distinct data shards. These trainers will then fine-tune a larger set of downstream 'practitioner' models. We will analyze the variance, generalization gap, and knowledge retention across tiers, comparing performance to a monolithic fine-tuning baseline and studying the 'parsing' mechanisms trainers develop to transfer task-specific knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for democratizing access to fine-tuned, specialized AI models in resource-constrained settings. It will contribute new theoretical insights into knowledge distillation in hierarchical systems and offer a practical methodology for accelerating the deployment of AI-assisted clinical tools in diverse healthcare environments.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation and federated learning exist, applying a structured public health intervention model (train-the-trainer) to design the communication topology and training regimen for a cascade of LLMs is a novel cross-disciplinary approach. It explicitly tests whether social learning principles can improve the efficiency of model tuning.

proposals.title: Simulation-Based Mastery Learning for Hepatitis C Treatment in Primary Care: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Improve Clinical Decision-Making and Patient Outcomes
proposals.motivation: The global burden of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) remains high, with significant treatment gaps in primary care settings. Primary care providers (PCPs) often lack the specialized training and confidence to manage complex HCV treatment regimens, including drug-drug interactions and monitoring for cure. This skills gap contributes to suboptimal patient care and delays in achieving elimination targets. High-fidelity simulation, proven effective in aviation and surgical training, offers a promising but under-explored method for procedural and clinical decision-making skill acquisition in primary care.
proposals.hypothesis: Primary care providers who undergo a simulation-based mastery learning (SBML) curriculum for HCV management will demonstrate superior clinical decision-making fidelity and achieve higher rates of sustained virologic response (SVR) in their patients, compared to PCPs receiving standard educational training.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial in primary health centers. The intervention arm will undergo an SBML curriculum using high-fidelity patient simulators and standardized patients to practice HCV treatment initiation, monitoring, and addressing complications, with required passing of a mastery check before treating real patients. The control arm will receive standard didactic training. The primary outcome will be patient-level SVR at 12 weeks post-treatment. Secondary outcomes include PCP performance on validated clinical scenario tests and treatment fidelity measures. We will use causal inference methods and mixed-effects models to analyze the impact, accounting for cluster design and patient-level covariates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide rigorous evidence for the efficacy of SBML in a non-procedural, complex chronic disease management context within primary care. It will yield a validated, scalable training protocol that can be adapted for other conditions, directly addressing the 'treatment gap' theme. The study will also contribute methodological insights into measuring and optimizing the 'generalization' of clinical skills from simulation to real-world practice.
proposals.novelty: While SBML is established in procedural specialties, its application to optimize the cognitive and decision-making workflows of primary care providers for a specific chronic disease is novel. The proposal uniquely integrates high-stakes clinical outcomes (SVR) with a training methodology from high-reliability industries, creating a new paradigm for closing competency gaps in decentralized healthcare delivery.

proposals.title: Iterative Process Optimization in Tele-Education: A PDSA Cycle Framework for Enhancing Treatment Fidelity in Project ECHO
proposals.motivation: Project ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes) has demonstrated efficacy in scaling specialty care, such as psychiatric treatment, to underserved areas via tele-education. However, pre-2026 studies, including Arora's work on reducing the treatment gap, highlight variability in implementation and treatment fidelity across ECHO sites. This variability can limit the model's impact and scalability, creating a need for systematic, data-driven methods to optimize local clinical processes.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle framework at ECHO hub-and-spoke sites will lead to measurable, iterative improvements in treatment process adherence and patient outcomes compared to standard ECHO implementation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the manufacturing PDSA cycle for healthcare quality improvement. At participating ECHO sites, local clinical teams will define a specific treatment process metric (Plan), implement a change (Do), and collect data via the ECHO platform. We will employ statistical process control charts to analyze time-series data (Study). Crucially, we will integrate this with a fine-tuning approach for a predictive model (e.g., a transformer-based architecture trained on ECHO session transcripts and patient outcome data) to recommend the most effective subsequent interventions (Act). This creates a closed-loop system where model recommendations inform the next PDSA cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, scalable framework for continuous quality improvement within the ECHO model, moving beyond static training to dynamic process optimization. It will yield a novel methodology combining healthcare operations research with machine learning fine-tuning, specifically tailored for distributed tele-education networks. The results will offer concrete evidence on whether iterative, data-driven adaptation can enhance the consistency and effectiveness of complex care delivery in low-resource settings.
proposals.novelty: While PDSA is known in healthcare, its integration into the ECHO tele-education model as a structured, algorithmically-informed feedback loop is novel. The key innovation is coupling the PDSA cycle with a fine-tuned language model that learns from iterative site data to recommend actionable improvements, transforming ECHO from a knowledge-dissemination platform into an adaptive learning system.

proposals.title: Co-Designing a Culturally Adapted ECHO Intervention for Mental Health: A Community-Based Participatory Research Framework
proposals.motivation: The treatment gap for psychiatric disorders in low-resource settings like South India remains vast, despite evidence for task-shifting models like the ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes) framework. Pre-2026 work shows that while ECHO can train community health workers (ASHAs), engagement and long-term effectiveness may be limited if the intervention's content and delivery are not culturally resonant. Community-Based Participatory Research (CBPR) principles, which center equitable partnership with communities, offer a promising but under-explored method to enhance relevance and impact in global mental health implementations.
proposals.hypothesis: That a mental health ECHO intervention for ASHAs, co-designed with patients and community stakeholders using structured CBPR principles, will yield a more culturally relevant curriculum and implementation strategy, leading to measurably higher community engagement and ASHA self-efficacy compared to a standard, expert-driven ECHO model.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-phase CBPR design in a South Indian district. First, we will form a Community Advisory Board (CAB) of patients, family members, ASHAs, and local leaders. Using iterative, structured workshops (informed by pre-2026 participatory design methods), the CAB will co-create core intervention modules. We will then formalize this co-designed content into a tailored ECHO curriculum. Its efficacy will be tested via a cluster-randomized controlled trial, comparing the co-designed ECHO arm against a standard ECHO arm. Primary outcomes will include ASHA competency (via validated scales) and community engagement metrics (attendance, qualitative feedback), analyzed using mixed-effects models accounting for cluster design.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, replicable framework for integrating CBPR into digital tele-mentoring platforms like ECHO, moving beyond tokenistic community input. It will generate empirical evidence on whether deep co-design improves implementation outcomes in global mental health. The resulting open-source, culturally adapted curriculum will be a direct contribution to reducing the psychiatric treatment gap.
proposals.novelty: While CBPR is established and ECHO is a proven training model, their integration for culturally adapting a *digital* tele-mentoring intervention for mental health is novel. The approach systematically translates participatory principles into a structured co-design process for an ECHO curriculum, testing its additive value with a rigorous comparative trial design not commonly applied to adaptation studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Preventing Disease Progression: An ECHO Model for Training Primary Care Providers in Complex Preventive Care for At-Risk Populations
proposals.motivation: Chronic diseases like diabetes and cardiovascular conditions are a leading global health burden, with a significant portion of this burden preventable through early, complex interventions in primary care. However, primary care providers (PCPs) often lack the specialized training and support to implement these strategies effectively, especially for at-risk populations, creating a major prevention gap. The ECHO (Extension for Community Healthcare Outcomes) model, proven for expanding treatment capacity in psychiatry and other specialties, offers a scalable, tele-mentoring framework but has been underutilized for proactive, multi-factorial disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured ECHO intervention, focused on complex preventive care protocols, will significantly improve PCPs' self-efficacy, knowledge, and implementation fidelity for preventive strategies, leading to measurable improvements in patient-level risk factors compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core ECHO model (hub-and-spoke knowledge networks, case-based learning, didactic sessions) to a preventive care curriculum. The intervention will be a randomized controlled trial where PCPs (spokes) are assigned to a 6-month Preventive Care ECHO series or a control group. We will use validated scales to measure PCP outcomes (self-efficacy, knowledge). Patient outcomes (e.g., biometric data, preventive service utilization) will be tracked via electronic health records. The analysis will employ mixed-effects models to account for clustering of patients within providers, comparing pre-post changes between groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous evidence for the ECHO model as a tool for primary prevention, shifting its application from treatment to proactive health. It will yield a replicable, curriculum-based framework for scaling complex preventive care competencies. The findings could directly inform health policy on workforce training to reduce the incidence and progression of chronic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While ECHO is established for treatment, its systematic application to *preventive* strategies, particularly those requiring nuanced clinical decision-making for at-risk patients, is novel. The study design explicitly tests if the model's collaborative learning structure can overcome the implementation gap for prevention, a distinct challenge from treatment delivery.

proposals.title: Beyond the Lazy Regime: The Neural Tangent Kernel under Adaptive and Large-Step Optimizers
proposals.motivation: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory provides a powerful framework for understanding the training dynamics of wide neural networks under gradient descent (GD) with infinitesimal learning rates. However, modern deep learning relies on adaptive optimizers like Adam and often uses large learning rates, regimes where the standard NTK assumptions break down. Understanding the effective kernel dynamics in these practical settings is crucial for a more accurate theory of deep learning generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adaptive optimizers and large learning rates induce a time-varying, data-dependent effective NTK that deviates significantly from the constant, initialization-dependent kernel predicted by lazy training theory, and that this deviation can be formally characterized and linked to generalization performance.
proposals.method: We will analyze the training dynamics of wide Transformers and fully-connected networks using tools from stochastic approximation and continuous-time limits. We will derive modified NTK flow equations for Adam and SGD with momentum, focusing on the role of preconditioning and second-moment estimates. For large learning rates, we will study the spectral properties of the kernel's evolution using Floquet theory, simulating training on standard NLP and vision benchmarks to empirically validate our theoretical predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend NTK theory to realistic optimization scenarios, providing a more nuanced understanding of how optimizer choice and hyperparameters shape the function space trajectory. It will offer theoretical grounding for empirical observations on optimizer-dependent generalization, bridging the gap between the 'lazy' and 'feature learning' regimes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies the NTK under gradient flow, our focus on adaptive optimizers and non-infinitesimal step sizes directly addresses the gap between theory and practice. The connection between optimizer-induced kernel dynamics and generalization, particularly for architectures like Transformers, remains largely unexplored.

proposals.title: Beyond the Static Kernel: Quantifying and Exploiting Neural Tangent Kernel Evolution in Finite-Width Transformers
proposals.motivation: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory provides a powerful framework for understanding infinite-width neural networks, where the kernel remains static during training, guaranteeing convergence. However, real-world models are finite-width, and empirical evidence suggests their feature representations evolve. This gap between theory and practice limits our understanding of generalization and optimization in models like Transformers. A precise characterization of NTK evolution in practical settings is crucial for developing more accurate theoretical models of deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in finite-width Transformer models trained on natural language tasks, the NTK undergoes significant, structured evolution that correlates with improved generalization, and that deliberately constraining or guiding this evolution can lead to more sample-efficient training.
proposals.method: We will analyze NTK dynamics during the fine-tuning of medium-scale Transformer language models (e.g., GPT-2 architecture variants) on downstream tasks from clinical text (linking to Arora's health informatics work). We will track the NTK's Frobenius norm and its top eigenvalues throughout training, correlating changes with validation performance. We will then design a novel training algorithm, 'Echo-Tune', that uses a running echo state of past gradient directions to compute a dynamic regularization term, gently penalizing NTK changes that hurt generalization on a held-out patient study subset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative study of NTK evolution in Transformer fine-tuning, bridging a key gap between infinite-width theory and empirical practice. The proposed 'Echo-Tune' algorithm will demonstrate that actively managing kernel dynamics can improve sample efficiency, offering a new perspective on optimization for language models in data-scarce domains like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes the NTK is not static, our approach systematically measures its evolution in modern architectures and actively exploits this dynamics for algorithmic improvement, moving beyond passive observation to controlled guidance.

proposals.title: The Deep CNTK Limit: Tractability, Generalization, and the Role of Fine-Tuning in Ultra-Deep Neural Kernels
proposals.motivation: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) and its Convolutional variant (CNTK) provide a powerful theoretical lens for understanding the training dynamics of infinitely wide networks. However, prior analyses are largely confined to shallow or moderately deep architectures. With the empirical success of very deep networks (e.g., ResNets with hundreds of layers), a critical gap exists: we lack a principled understanding of the behavior, stability, and computational tractability of the CNTK in this ultra-deep regime, which is essential for connecting theory to modern practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ultra-deep CNTK limit, when properly normalized and parameterized, remains computationally tractable and reveals a spectral bias that explains the generalization benefits of depth, analogous to the implicit regularization seen in local SGD methods for deep models.
proposals.method: We will extend the recursive kernel formulation for CNTKs to depths of hundreds of layers, incorporating skip connections as in Wide ResNets. We will develop efficient numerical schemes, potentially using stochastic approximations or low-rank factorizations, to compute the kernel matrix and its eigenvalues for large-scale datasets like CIFAR-10. The analysis will focus on the evolution of the kernel spectrum during training and fine-tuning phases, drawing methodological parallels to the study of optimization dynamics in prior work on SGD.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first rigorous framework for analyzing ultra-deep networks through the kernel lens, providing concrete predictions about their trainability and generalization. It will deliver practical algorithms for approximating deep CNTKs, enabling their use as priors or for understanding architectural choices. The findings will bridge the gap between infinite-width theory and finite, ultra-deep practice.
proposals.novelty: While CNTKs are known, their systematic study at depths of hundreds of layers is unexplored territory. The novelty lies in scaling the theoretical machinery to modern architectural scales and explicitly linking the deep CNTK's spectral properties to empirical phenomena like the benefits of depth and fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Beyond Gaussianity: A Theoretical and Empirical Analysis of Optimization and Generalization for Structured, Non-IID Data
proposals.motivation: Theoretical analyses of optimization algorithms like SGD and Local SGD often rely on the simplifying assumption of isotropic Gaussian input data. This fails to capture the structured, non-IID, and often heavy-tailed distributions found in real-world domains such as natural language (echo, language) and healthcare (patients, health). Understanding training dynamics under realistic data geometries is crucial for explaining generalization gaps and improving model tuning for specific applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative generalization performance of distributed methods like Local SGD versus standard SGD is fundamentally altered by input data structure and non-Gaussianity, and that this interaction can be characterized by new, distribution-dependent measures of stability and sharpness.
proposals.method: We will extend the theoretical framework from Arora's work on Local SGD generalization to structured distributions, including mixtures and distributions with covariance structure derived from real text (trained Transformers) and health record data. Empirically, we will measure optimization trajectories, loss landscapes, and generalization gaps on controlled synthetic data and real benchmarks, using fine-tuning protocols to isolate the effect of data distribution shifts. The analysis will link performance to data-dependent quantities like the effective dimension and spectral decay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more rigorous foundation for comparing optimization algorithms in practical settings, moving beyond idealized assumptions. It will yield concrete guidance on when methods like Local SGD are beneficial for non-IID data, directly impacting distributed training for language models and personalized health model tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior theory assumes Gaussian or isotropic data, we explicitly connect data geometry from real-world domains (language, health) to optimization theory. The integration of theoretical stability analysis with empirical study of structured distributions from pre-2026 models and datasets is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Kernel Dynamics of Deep Neural Networks: A Path to Understanding Feature Evolution in Multi-Layer Architectures
proposals.motivation: The Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory provides a powerful framework for analyzing the training dynamics of infinitely-wide networks, but its application has been largely confined to shallow (e.g., two-layer) architectures. As real-world models like deep Transformers are central to modern AI, a critical gap exists in our theoretical understanding of how feature representations evolve during gradient descent in deep, finite-width networks. Extending the kernel-based dynamics framework to deeper networks is essential for explaining generalization, the role of depth in representation learning, and the efficacy of fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the gradient flow dynamics of deep, finite-width neural networks can be characterized by a hierarchy of data-dependent, evolving kernels, where the kernel for each layer is a function of the kernels and activations of preceding layers, and that this formulation will reveal depth-specific phases of feature learning.
proposals.method: We will extend the mean-field and perturbative analysis techniques used for two-layer networks to architectures with L>2 layers. The core approach involves deriving coupled differential equations for the evolution of the weight matrices and the associated inter-layer covariance kernels Σ^(l)(t) across layers l=1,...,L. We will employ Tensor Programs or similar methods to track the high-dimensional statistics, focusing on ReLU or GeLU activations common in Transformers. The analysis will be validated empirically by training controlled deep networks (e.g., deep MLPs) on synthetic and standard vision/language tasks, comparing the theoretical kernel predictions to measured layer-wise feature changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive theoretical framework for the end-to-end training dynamics of deep, finite-width networks under gradient descent, moving beyond the infinite-width limit. It will yield testable predictions about how depth influences the rate and stability of feature learning, potentially explaining phenomena like deep signal propagation and the benefits of pre-training and fine-tuning in deep models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes either infinite-width deep NTKs (static) or dynamics of two-layer nets, our proposal uniquely tackles the coupled, non-linear evolution of interacting kernels in deep, finite-width networks, a significantly more complex and realistic regime that is not a straightforward extension of existing shallow theory.

proposals.title: Beyond Squared Loss: A Theoretical and Empirical Study of Optimization Dynamics for Robust and Structured Losses
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning, particularly in language models and healthcare applications, relies on complex, non-convex optimization. The vast majority of theoretical analyses, however, focus on simplified settings like squared loss, which poorly captures losses used in practice (e.g., cross-entropy for language, robust losses for noisy health data). Understanding the training dynamics for these practical losses is crucial for explaining generalization, as hinted by Arora's work on Local SGD, and for designing efficient, reliable algorithms for sensitive domains like mental health prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimization dynamics for robust losses (e.g., Huber, log-cosh) and structured losses (e.g., cross-entropy with label smoothing) induce fundamentally different implicit regularization and convergence properties compared to squared loss, leading to models with better generalization and calibration, especially in the presence of label noise or distribution shift.
proposals.method: We will conduct a joint theoretical and empirical study. Theoretically, we will analyze gradient flow/descent dynamics for a two-layer ReLU network under a teacher-student setup, comparing squared loss with robust alternatives. Empirically, we will train transformer language models (e.g., GPT-2 architecture) and convolutional networks on medical imaging datasets (e.g., CheXpert) using different loss functions. We will meticulously track metrics like sharpness of minima, gradient noise structure, and performance on in-distribution and out-of-distribution test sets, linking these to theoretical predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for analyzing optimization with non-standard losses, bridging a gap between theory and practice. It will offer concrete guidance on loss function selection for improving model robustness and generalization in critical applications like clinical prediction, directly informed by optimization dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has empirically evaluated different losses, a systematic theoretical analysis of their optimization dynamics in modern non-convex settings is lacking. This proposal uniquely connects the study of loss surfaces (an optimization focus) with practical outcomes in healthcare and language modeling (an applications focus), a synthesis aligned with Arora's recent interdisciplinary research trajectory.

proposals.title: Data-Centric Optimization: Designing Training Sets for Fast Convergence via Data-Dependent Complexity Measures
proposals.motivation: Training large-scale models like Transformers is computationally expensive, and data selection strategies (e.g., curriculum learning) are often heuristic. Recent theoretical work, including Arora's on data-dependent generalization bounds for SGD, provides a principled framework for quantifying how data influences training dynamics. This project aims to invert that analysis: instead of analyzing a given dataset, we actively design or select data to minimize a complexity measure, thereby accelerating training.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating an optimization problem that minimizes a data-dependent PAC-Bayes or Rademacher complexity measure over a candidate data pool, we can algorithmically construct training subsets or sequences that lead to provably faster convergence in neural network training, particularly for language models.
proposals.method: We will operationalize complexity measures from recent non-uniform data-dependent generalization bounds. For a given model architecture (e.g., Transformer) and task, we will define an objective that estimates the complexity for any candidate data batch. Using this as a loss, we will develop an iterative algorithm—potentially inspired by coreset selection or Bayesian optimization—to select or re-weight training examples from a large pool. The method will be tested by comparing the training curves (loss vs. steps) of standard SGD and Local SGD against our data-designed schedules on benchmark NLP tasks and a health outcome prediction task using patient notes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a theoretically grounded, automated alternative to heuristic curriculum learning. It will yield practical algorithms for data selection that reduce wall-clock training time. Furthermore, it will deepen the understanding of the interplay between data geometry, optimization, and generalization by testing theoretical complexity measures in an active design setting.
proposals.novelty: While data pruning and curriculum learning exist, using data-dependent generalization bounds as an objective to *inversely design* training data is a novel, optimization-centric perspective. Bridging this theoretical analysis with the empirical study of training dynamics in Transformers and health prediction models is also unexplored.

proposals.title: De-escalating Ribavirin in HCV Therapy: A Risk-Stratified Clinical Trial Model Inspired by Oncology
proposals.motivation: Direct-acting antiviral (DAA) regimens for hepatitis C virus (HCV) achieve sustained virologic response (SVR) rates >95%, but many include ribavirin, which causes significant hemolytic anemia and other toxicities. This reduces tolerability and complicates management, particularly in resource-limited settings. The success of de-escalation trials in oncology, where toxic adjuvant chemotherapy is safely omitted for biomarker-defined subgroups after effective primary treatments, provides a powerful analog for challenging the blanket necessity of ribavirin.
proposals.hypothesis: A biomarker-defined subgroup of HCV patients (e.g., IL28B CC genotype, low baseline viral load, mild fibrosis) treated with a ribavirin-free DAA regimen will achieve non-inferior SVR rates compared to the standard ribavirin-containing regimen, while demonstrating significantly improved safety and tolerability.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized, controlled non-inferiority trial. Patients will be risk-stratified using pre-treatment biomarkers (IL28B genotype, viral load, fibrosis score). Eligible 'low-risk' patients will be randomized to receive either the standard-of-care DAA+ribavirin regimen or an experimental ribavirin-free DAA regimen. The primary endpoint is SVR at 12 weeks post-treatment. Secondary endpoints include incidence of anemia, quality-of-life measures, and treatment discontinuation rates. Statistical analysis will employ intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses to test for non-inferiority in efficacy and superiority in safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous clinical framework for personalizing and simplifying HCV therapy, potentially establishing a new standard of care for a large patient subgroup. It will demonstrate the translational value of cross-disciplinary analogies, specifically from oncology de-escalation, to infectious disease therapeutic optimization.
proposals.novelty: While DAA optimization is an active area, this approach is novel in its explicit application of a structured de-escalation paradigm from oncology, moving beyond simple efficacy comparisons to a biomarker-driven, risk-adapted trial design focused on reducing toxicity while preserving cure.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework to Identify Patient Subgroups for Ribavirin-Sparing Hepatitis C Treatment: A Cost-Effectiveness and Efficacy Analysis
proposals.motivation: Direct-acting antiviral (DAA) regimens for Hepatitis C virus (HCV) achieve sustained virologic response (SVR) rates >95%, yet ribavirin is often added in specific scenarios, adding significant toxicity and cost. Given the high baseline efficacy, the marginal benefit of ribavirin is unclear for many patients, leading to unnecessary side effects and reduced treatment adherence. A data-driven method to precisely identify which patients truly require ribavirin is needed to optimize care and resource allocation in global health settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying fine-tuning and interpretability methods to electronic health record (EHR) and clinical trial data, we can identify a large subgroup of HCV patients for whom ribavirin confers no significant improvement in SVR, and that treating this subgroup without ribavirin would be non-inferior in efficacy and more cost-effective.
proposals.method: We will train a transformer-based model on sequential EHR data (demographics, lab values, fibrosis scores, prior treatment history) from patients treated with ribavirin-containing DAA regimens to predict SVR. Using attention mechanisms, we will parse which patient features the model associates with successful outcomes. We will then apply a novel constrained optimization algorithm, inspired by local SGD analysis for generalization, to 'ablate' the ribavirin effect in the model and simulate outcomes. This will define a candidate subgroup. We will validate this subgroup using a retrospective cohort study, comparing observed SVR rates in matched patients who received ribavirin versus those who did not.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, interpretable machine learning framework for personalizing HCV therapy, directly reducing the treatment gap by making therapy safer and more accessible. It will contribute a new methodology for 'virtual clinical trials' using model ablation to test treatment necessity. The findings could inform clinical guidelines to deprescribe ribavirin for specific patient populations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies factors for ribavirin use, our approach novelly applies model interpretability and ablation techniques from NLP to a clinical optimization problem, framing ribavirin necessity as a feature attribution challenge. It moves beyond association to simulate counterfactual treatment scenarios using pre-existing data.

proposals.title: Adaptive, Response-Guided Treatment Duration: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Personalized Medicine Using Continuous Biomarker Streams
proposals.motivation: Current clinical practice and trial designs rely on fixed-duration treatment protocols, which fail to account for individual patient heterogeneity in treatment response and tolerability. This leads to unnecessary drug exposure, increased toxicity, and suboptimal outcomes. A paradigm shift towards adaptive, data-driven treatment duration is urgently needed to personalize care, especially in chronic and oncological diseases where treatment length critically impacts quality of life and cost.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning (RL) agent, trained on continuous streams of biomarker and patient-reported outcome data, can learn an optimal, individualized stopping policy that maximizes a composite reward function of efficacy and tolerability, outperforming fixed-duration and simple rule-based strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop a contextual bandit and deep RL framework (e.g., Deep Q-Networks or Policy Gradients) where the 'state' is a patient's temporal trajectory of biomarkers (e.g., viral load, tumor size from imaging, symptom scores) and tolerability metrics. The 'action' is to continue or stop treatment. The reward function will be carefully designed using clinical input to balance efficacy (e.g., sustained biomarker suppression) against negative effects (e.g., adverse events, cost). We will train and validate the model using high-frequency data from existing longitudinal cohort studies and adaptive trial simulations, ensuring the decision rules are interpretable and clinically actionable.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a generalizable, algorithmic platform for designing response-guided treatment trials and a software tool for simulating optimal stopping policies. It will provide a formal, data-driven framework to replace heuristic 'treat-to-target' approaches, directly contributing to the 'Reducing the Treatment Gap' agenda by enabling more efficient and personalized care delivery, even in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been explored for dose selection, its application to determining optimal *treatment duration* from continuous, multi-modal biomarker streams is novel. The integration of patient-reported tolerability data in real-time decision-making moves beyond purely physiological biomarkers, creating a holistic stopping rule that aligns with the patient's lived experience.

proposals.title: Optimizing Treatment Duration for Mental Health Interventions via Adaptive, Patient-Specific Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Standardized treatment durations (e.g., 12 or 24 weeks) for psychotherapy and digital interventions are often based on clinical trial protocols, not individual patient trajectories. This one-size-fits-all approach can lead to undertreatment or inefficient resource use, particularly in low-resource settings where community health workers (like ASHAs) deliver care. Pre-2026, machine learning has shown promise in personalizing healthcare, but its application to dynamically optimizing the duration and intensity of non-pharmacological treatments remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning (RL) agent, trained on longitudinal patient outcome data, can learn to recommend personalized, non-standard treatment durations (e.g., 8 or 16 weeks) that lead to superior or equivalent clinical outcomes compared to fixed-duration protocols, while improving resource efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a contextual bandit and later a full RL model (e.g., Deep Q-Network) using historical patient data, including baseline severity, engagement metrics, and weekly symptom scores. The agent's action space will be to continue or terminate treatment at each decision point (e.g., every 2 weeks). The reward function will combine clinical improvement and a cost for extended treatment. We will pre-train the model on existing trial data and then fine-tune it using offline RL methods to ensure safety before potential deployment in a simulation study.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational framework for personalizing treatment duration, moving beyond fixed schedules. It will yield a validated policy that can be integrated into digital health platforms to guide community health workers or therapists. The project bridges Arora's interests in algorithmic generalization and efficient training with a direct application to reducing the mental health treatment gap.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been proposed for treatment selection, its use to dynamically determine the *duration* of a psychosocial intervention is novel. The approach uniquely combines fine-tuning of pre-trained policy models on limited clinical data with a focus on constraints relevant to task-shifted care delivered by non-specialists.

proposals.title: A Causal Taxonomy for Treatment Non-Response: A Machine Learning Framework for Stratified Retreatment
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in precision medicine is therapeutic non-response, where patients are often retreated uniformly or trialed on new therapies without understanding the biological cause of initial failure. This leads to wasted resources and patient harm. Borrowing from reliability engineering, we posit that non-response, like system failure, has distinct, classifiable root causes that demand specific corrective actions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multimodal patient data (genomic, transcriptomic, clinical, pharmacokinetic) can be used to train a transformer-based model to reliably classify the mechanistic failure mode of initial treatment non-response, and that retreatment strategies selected based on this classification will show superior efficacy in silico and in retrospective cohorts compared to a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.method: We will develop a diagnostic framework using a multi-task transformer architecture fine-tuned on patient data. The model will be trained to perform two primary tasks: 1) a primary classification into a predefined 'failure taxonomy' (e.g., inadequate exposure, target alteration, compensatory pathway activation), and 2) a generative task to predict the most promising next-line therapy from a knowledge base. Training will leverage pre-2026 self-supervised learning on large-scale biomedical corpora (e.g., PubMed, clinical notes) for representation, followed by supervised fine-tuning on curated datasets of treatment non-responders with known outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, causal framework for understanding therapeutic resistance, moving beyond correlative biomarkers. It will yield a concrete, interpretable tool for clinicians to stratify non-responders and select rational retreatment. The resulting 'failure taxonomy' and model could be generalized across therapeutic domains, fundamentally changing the approach to salvage therapy.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the formal adaptation of Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) from engineering to clinical decision-making, framing non-response as a diagnosable event with discrete causes. Methodologically, it applies advanced language model fine-tuning not for general prediction, but for the specific task of causal mechanism classification in treatment failure.

proposals.title: Personalizing Retreatment Duration for Non-Responders: A Mechanism-Adaptive Framework Using Longitudinal Biomarker Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Fixed-duration retreatment regimens for patients who have failed prior therapy are often inefficient and ineffective, leading to prolonged morbidity, increased costs, and antimicrobial or antiviral resistance. In pre-2026 clinical practice, personalization focuses primarily on drug choice, not on optimizing the critical time-exposure dimension based on the biological mechanism of prior failure. This gap is especially pronounced in resource-constrained settings where efficient, tailored strategies are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct longitudinal biomarker trajectories (e.g., viral load decay patterns) during initial treatment failure define patient phenotypes for which mechanism-adaptive retreatment durations will yield superior efficacy and efficiency compared to a fixed-duration approach.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-frequency longitudinal patient data (e.g., viral load, transcriptomics) from prior non-responder cohorts to train a transformer-based sequence model that parses and classifies failure mechanisms (e.g., rapid rebound vs. slow decay). This model will serve as a feature extractor. We will then apply a causal meta-learning framework, building on concepts from local SGD generalization analysis, to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (HTEs) for different retreatment durations. The algorithm will output a policy mapping failure phenotypes to optimized durations (e.g., 8-week intensive vs. 16-week augmented), which will be validated in a simulated trial using inverse probability weighting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for personalizing not just the 'what' but the 'how long' of therapy, directly translating biological failure mechanisms into actionable clinical timelines. It will yield a concrete, interpretable decision tool for defining retreatment pathways, aiming to improve outcomes and conserve resources in complex patient populations.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive treatment strategies exist, the explicit coupling of mechanistic failure phenotypes derived from sequence models to personalized *duration*—a continuous, high-stakes clinical variable—is a novel and non-obvious extension of precision medicine beyond drug selection.

proposals.title: Blinded Adjudication in Open-Label Trials: A Computational Framework to Isolate Treatment Effect from Expectancy Bias
proposals.motivation: Open-label trials are often necessary in psychiatry and chronic disease management where blinding is ethically or practically impossible, yet they are vulnerable to significant bias from patient and provider expectations, confounding the true treatment effect. This bias inflates effect sizes in patient-reported outcomes and adherence metrics, undermining evidence-based care decisions. A method to objectively assess efficacy within a non-blinded design is a critical unmet need in health research.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a blinded endpoint adjudication layer for key digital and patient-reported outcomes within an open-label trial will yield significantly different, and more conservative, estimates of treatment efficacy compared to the primary open-label analysis, by mitigating the influence of expectancy bias on the final adjudicated result.
proposals.method: We will design a randomized controlled trial in a psychiatric disorder (e.g., depression) using a digital platform for patient diaries and sensor-based adherence tracking. Core efficacy data will be processed through a pipeline that anonymizes and strips treatment-arm labels before presentation to the independent endpoint adjudication committee. We will employ causal inference methods, specifically using the adjudicated blinded outcome as an instrumental variable or in a difference-in-differences framework, to quantify the bias component in the open-label assessment. Statistical models will be fine-tuned to account for covariates while preserving the adjudication blind.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical methodological framework for enhancing the internal validity of open-label trials, directly addressing a major limitation in comparative effectiveness research. It will yield quantitative estimates of expectancy bias magnitude in specific therapeutic areas and offer a blueprint for regulatory science on hybrid trial designs that balance pragmatism with rigor.
proposals.novelty: While blinded adjudication committees exist, their application is typically for hard endpoints (e.g., mortality, imaging). The novelty lies in systematically applying this 'double-blind audition' principle to soft, digitally captured endpoints within an open-label patient interaction, creating a hybrid design that decouples experience from evaluation.

proposals.title: Disentangling Expectation from Efficacy: A Deep Sequencing Framework to Quantify Open-Label Bias in Antiviral Trials
proposals.motivation: Open-label trial designs, while pragmatic, are confounded by expectation bias, where knowledge of treatment assignment influences patient-reported outcomes and adherence. This bias is particularly problematic in psychiatry and infectious diseases, where subjective endpoints are common, but it has been impossible to objectively quantify its magnitude separate from true pharmacological effect. Pre-2026 deep sequencing allows for sensitive detection of pre-existing minority drug-resistant viral variants, providing a novel biological signal to address this.
proposals.hypothesis: In an open-label antiviral trial, the differential expansion dynamics of pre-treatment minority resistant variants—a biological signal independent of patient perception—will correlate with and can be used to calibrate the magnitude of expectation bias influencing clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will design a trial with deep sequencing of baseline samples to quantify minority resistant variants. We will train a transformer-based model on viral population dynamics from blinded controlled studies to establish a baseline 'pharmacological effect' prediction. This model will then be applied to the open-label arm, where patient-reported outcomes and adherence are tracked. The discrepancy between the predicted (biological) and observed (clinical) outcomes, conditioned on variant dynamics and adherence data, will be modeled using a causal inference framework to isolate the bias component attributable to the open-label knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first methodological framework to objectively quantify open-label bias using hard biological data, moving beyond purely subjective corrections. It will yield a calibration tool for interpreting real-world evidence from open-label studies and inform more accurate efficacy estimates in pragmatic trials.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is using pre-existing minority variant dynamics as an internal, perception-independent comparator—a 'molecular tracer' for drug effect—to disentangle expectation bias from true efficacy, a fusion of deep sequencing and causal modeling not previously applied to trial design bias.

proposals.title: Quantifying Open-Label Bias in Digital Mental Health Interventions: A Causal Inference and Language Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Open-label designs are common in pragmatic trials of digital mental health tools, but they introduce significant potential for bias in patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and adherence. Pre-2026 research highlights the critical role of patient engagement and the treatment gap in psychiatry, yet the specific mechanisms and magnitude of bias introduced by patients' knowledge of treatment assignment remain poorly quantified. This limits the validity of efficacy assessments for scalable interventions, such as those delivered by community health workers.
proposals.hypothesis: In trials of therapist-assisted digital interventions, the open-label knowledge of receiving active support introduces a significant positive bias in PROs, mediated by changes in patient language use in therapeutic interactions and self-reports, which can be disentangled from the specific therapeutic protocol's effect.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing datasets from open-label trials of digital cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) with human support. Using transformer-based language models (e.g., BERT, GPT-2) fine-tuned on clinical text, we will extract linguistic embeddings from patient-therapist message exchanges and patient diary entries. We will then train a causal forest model to estimate the conditional average treatment effect (CATE) of the open-label condition on PROs, using the linguistic features, adherence metrics, and baseline patient characteristics as covariates. This will isolate the bias component from the protocol effect by comparing linguistic shifts in open-label vs. blinded sub-studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for adjusting efficacy estimates in open-label mental health studies. It will contribute to methodological rigor in global mental health research by identifying linguistic markers of expectancy bias, ultimately leading to more accurate assessments of interventions designed to reduce the treatment gap.
proposals.novelty: While the existence of open-label bias is known, this proposal novelly applies modern NLP and causal machine learning techniques from theoretical computer science (e.g., analysis of generalization in SGD) to parse and quantify its specific linguistic pathways in a mental health context, moving beyond simple statistical adjustment.

proposals.title: Hypergraph Partitioning for Cohort Design: Identifying Critical Subgroups to Optimize Personalized HCV Treatment Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Despite the high efficacy of ledipasvir-sofosbuvir for HCV, treatment response can vary across patient subpopulations defined by complex, overlapping clinical and social factors. Traditional trial stratification often fails to capture these high-dimensional interactions, leaving ambiguity about personalized efficacy. This creates a 'treatment gap' where optimal regimens for complex, real-world patients remain unclear, echoing challenges in global mental health where non-specialist workers must navigate heterogeneous populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patient subgroups which are computationally most difficult to isolate via hypergraph partitioning—those with the highest multi-factorial interconnectivity—represent the critical subpopulations where treatment effect estimates are most uncertain, and that targeting these clusters in trial analysis will maximize information gain for personalized treatment rules.
proposals.method: We will model a cohort of treatment-naive HCV patients as a hypergraph, with patients as nodes and hyperedges defined by shared characteristics (e.g., genotype, IL28B polymorphism, fibrosis score, comorbidities). We will apply spectral methods and multi-commodity flow-based algorithms (e.g., expander flows, inspired by Arora-Rao-Vazirani) to partition this graph. The 'hardness' of isolating a cluster will be quantified by the conductance of its cut. We will then evaluate treatment outcomes within these identified, tightly interconnected clusters to assess heterogeneity of treatment effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, graph-theoretic framework for data-driven cohort partitioning in clinical research, moving beyond single-factor stratification. It will deliver a concrete algorithm to identify patient subgroups where clinical evidence is most ambiguous, directly informing targeted enrollment or stratified analysis. The methodology bridges theoretical computer science and pragmatic clinical trial design, aiming to reduce the evaluation gap for personalized regimens.
proposals.novelty: The application of advanced expander graph and multi-commodity flow partitioning techniques to model patient similarity for clinical trial design is novel. It frames the problem of identifying critical subgroups not through standard regression, but through the computational lens of graph connectivity and separation hardness.

proposals.title: Fine-Tuning Transformer Models for Personalized Treatment Regimen Assessment in Patients with Advanced Cirrhosis
proposals.motivation: Advanced cirrhosis represents a major global health burden with high mortality, yet treatment efficacy varies significantly across patient subgroups. Current clinical decision support tools lack the sophistication to model complex, non-linear interactions between patient comorbidities, medication regimens, and longitudinal outcomes. Leveraging recent advances in language models for structured clinical data offers a promising path to more personalized care.
proposals.hypothesis: Transformer-based models, fine-tuned on multimodal electronic health records (EHRs), can parse temporal patient histories to identify subtle predictors of treatment efficacy and safety for specific regimens in advanced cirrhosis, outperforming traditional statistical models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained transformer architecture (e.g., BERT or ClinicalBERT) using a two-stage fine-tuning process. First, domain adaptation will be performed via masked language modeling on a large corpus of de-identified clinical notes and structured EHR data from patients with liver disease. Second, task-specific tuning will train the model to predict a composite outcome of regimen efficacy and safety, using longitudinal patient trajectories as input sequences. We will employ techniques like local SGD to improve generalization from distributed hospital data and analyze attention maps to interpret the model's 'parsing' of critical clinical events.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, interpretable AI tool for assessing cirrhosis treatment regimens. It will contribute methodologically by demonstrating how to adapt and tune large language models for complex, longitudinal clinical prediction tasks where data is heterogeneous and high-dimensional.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the parsing capabilities of transformers for temporal clinical data with a focus on a high-morbidity, under-studied population (advanced cirrhosis), moving beyond diagnostic NLP to predictive regimen optimization.

proposals.title: Constraint-Relaxed Semidefinite Programming for Modeling HCV Genotype 1b Kinetics and Optimizing Ledipasvir-Sofosbuvir Therapy
proposals.motivation: Despite the high efficacy of direct-acting antivirals like ledipasvir-sofosbuvir for HCV genotype 1b, predicting individual treatment outcomes remains challenging due to complex, high-dimensional viral-host-drug interactions. Traditional pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) models often fail to capture these nonlinear dynamics, leading to suboptimal, one-size-fits-all regimens. An optimization framework that can integrate diverse patient data to model these interactions and suggest personalized protocols is urgently needed to maximize sustained virologic response (SVR) rates while minimizing treatment duration and cost.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a semidefinite programming (SDP) framework, with strategically relaxed triangle inequality constraints, can more accurately model the genotype-specific viral kinetic landscape than standard PK/PD models, and that the optimal solution of this SDP will reveal predictive immunological-virological signatures for treatment success, enabling the design of optimized, shorter-duration regimens.
proposals.method: We will formulate patient-specific viral kinetic states and treatment responses as a high-dimensional optimization over a positive semidefinite matrix, where entries represent interactions between virological, immunological, and drug concentration variables. Key triangle inequality constraints in the SDP, which may over-constrain biological reality, will be relaxed using a weighted L1-penalty term. We will solve this using a first-order optimization algorithm (e.g., an augmented Lagrangian method) on clinical trial data, including serial HCV RNA, cytokine levels, and patient demographics. The resulting model will be used to simulate and optimize treatment duration against the objective of achieving SVR.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-driven mathematical framework for HCV treatment personalization, moving beyond classical PK/PD modeling. It will provide a method to identify multivariate predictive biomarkers of treatment response from complex patient data. Ultimately, it aims to generate testable, optimized treatment protocols that could reduce the standard 12-week course for eligible genotype 1b patients, lowering economic burden.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of clinical hepatology with advanced optimization theory, specifically applying constraint-relaxed SDP—a technique explored in theoretical computer science for metric learning and clustering—to the domain of viral kinetics and treatment optimization, which is a non-obvious cross-disciplinary application.

proposals.title: Adversarial Retention and Bias-Corrected Estimation for Long-Term Outcomes in Real-World Hepatitis C Treatment Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Real-world evidence on the durability of hepatitis C cure (SVR) and long-term clinical outcomes is often compromised by substantial attrition bias, as patients lost to follow-up are systematically different from those retained. This leads to over-optimistic estimates of treatment effectiveness and masks disparities in outcomes across vulnerable subgroups. A principled, pre-specified methodology to proactively minimize and analytically correct for this bias is critically needed to inform public health policy and clinical care.
proposals.hypothesis: A proactive, multi-modal patient retention system, combined with a pre-specified missing data model that treats loss-to-follow-up as an adversarial selection process, will yield significantly different and more reliable estimates of SVR durability, hepatocellular carcinoma incidence, and all-cause mortality compared to standard complete-case analyses in a real-world antiviral therapy cohort.
proposals.method: We will implement a proactive retention system using community health workers (ASHAs) as patient navigators, mobile health reminders, and flexible visit scheduling. To model attrition, we frame loss-to-follow-up as an adversarial covariate shift problem. We pre-specify a suite of estimators, including targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) and doubly robust methods, using gradient-boosted machines to model the missingness mechanism. The analysis will treat subgroups defined by baseline characteristics as distinct 'tasks', applying ideas from multi-task learning to share statistical strength while estimating subgroup-specific bias corrections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, bias-corrected assessment of long-term HCV treatment outcomes in a real-world South Asian setting, directly informing elimination strategies. Methodologically, it will advance the application of adversarial robustness and semi-parametric estimation frameworks to the problem of attrition bias in longitudinal clinical studies, creating a replicable analysis pipeline.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively combines a proactive, human-centered retention intervention with a pre-registered analytical strategy that formally treats missingness as an adversarial covariate shift, moving beyond standard sensitivity analyses to provide primary corrected estimates.

proposals.title: Modeling Long-Term Clinical Outcomes Post-SVR: A Machine Learning Framework for Risk Stratification and Durability Prediction
proposals.motivation: The advent of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) has made sustained virologic response (SVR) achievable for most hepatitis C patients. However, pre-2026, long-term follow-up data on the durability of SVR and associated clinical outcomes (e.g., hepatocellular carcinoma, decompensation) remains limited, especially in diverse, real-world populations. Understanding long-term trajectories is critical for patient counseling, health system planning, and optimizing post-SVR monitoring protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on longitudinal, multi-modal patient data (demographics, treatment history, lab values, comorbidities) can accurately stratify patients by their risk of late virologic relapse or adverse clinical outcomes following SVR, and that these risk profiles are non-uniform across populations.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large-scale, longitudinal electronic health record dataset from a multi-site cohort with long-term follow-up. Inspired by recent work on fine-tuning and training dynamics in language models, we will adapt transformer-based architectures (e.g., BERT or clinicalBERT) for sequential modeling of patient timelines. The model will be trained using a masked prediction objective on historical data and fine-tuned for specific downstream tasks: time-to-event analysis for clinical outcomes and binary classification for late virologic failure. We will employ techniques like Local SGD to improve generalization across heterogeneous patient subgroups, akin to its benefits in distributed, non-IID settings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven framework for predicting long-term post-SVR outcomes, moving beyond population-level estimates to individualized risk assessment. It will contribute a novel application of sequence modeling architectures to longitudinal clinical prognosis and offer insights into the factors governing the durability of treatment success in a real-world setting.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies report SVR rates, applying advanced sequence models (transformers) fine-tuned for long-term clinical risk prediction post-SVR is novel. The integration of methods from distributed optimization (Local SGD) to handle cohort heterogeneity directly addresses a key challenge in generalizing models across diverse patient populations, a gap in existing clinical ML work.

proposals.title: Personalized Mental Health Intervention via Constrained Bandits on a Bayesian Digital Phenotype
proposals.motivation: Adaptive trials in mental health often rely on infrequent, coarse clinical endpoints, creating a mismatch with the dynamic, multi-faceted nature of psychiatric illness and treatment response. This limits the ability to personalize care in real-time based on a patient's current symptom state, functioning, and side-effect burden. High-frequency digital phenotyping from smartphones offers a rich data stream but lacks a principled framework to directly guide adaptive intervention allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: A constrained multi-armed bandit algorithm, whose reward signal is a personalized composite utility score inferred from a Bayesian model of continuous digital phenotype data, will yield more effective and acceptable intervention allocations than standard adaptive designs using only periodic clinical assessments.
