proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model to translate multimodal smartphone sensor data and ecological momentary assessments (EMAs) into a dynamic, personalized utility score integrating core symptoms, functioning, and side effects. This score will serve as the reward for a constrained linear contextual bandit algorithm (e.g., LinUCB with safety constraints) that allocates patients to different intervention components (e.g., behavioral activation prompts, mindfulness exercises) in real-time. Hard safety rules, defined a priori with clinicians, will override allocations that risk harm. The system will be validated in a pilot sequential multiple assignment randomized trial (SMART) for depression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for closing the loop between continuous digital phenotyping and adaptive intervention delivery. It contributes a new method for patient-centered trial design that optimizes for a composite, personalized outcome in real-time. The results will advance the field of digital mental health towards truly context-aware and dynamically personalized care.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct integration of a probabilistically inferred, multi-dimensional digital phenotype as the reward signal for a constrained bandit, moving beyond using raw sensor data or simple summaries. This creates a patient-centered adaptation logic grounded in a holistic, dynamic clinical state, which is non-trivial given the noise and complexity of the data.

proposals.title: Thompson Sampling for Patient-Centric Adaptive Trials: A Multi-Armed Bandit Framework for Psychiatric Treatment Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Traditional randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are statistically rigid and ethically suboptimal, as they allocate many patients to potentially inferior treatments. This is especially critical in global mental health, where treatment gaps are vast and resources are limited, as seen in work on community health workers in South India. Adaptive designs promise greater efficiency but require robust, real-time decision engines to manage the exploration-exploitation trade-off inherent in evaluating multiple novel interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a clinical trial design using Thompson Sampling, a Bayesian multi-armed bandit algorithm, as its allocation rule will yield superior aggregate patient outcomes (higher cumulative reward) and faster, more statistically efficient identification of the best treatment, compared to standard fixed-randomization or simpler adaptive designs, in a simulated psychiatric treatment setting.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment modeling a multi-arm trial for a psychiatric disorder (e.g., depression), with treatment arms as bandits and patient binary response (e.g., remission) as stochastic rewards. The core algorithm will be a contextual Thompson Sampling bandit, where patient baseline features (from a simplified EHR) provide context. We will implement a Bayesian logistic regression model for each arm's reward probability, updating posteriors after each patient outcome. Allocation for each new patient will be determined by sampling from these posteriors and selecting the arm with the highest sampled success probability. Performance will be benchmarked against standard 1:1 randomization and epsilon-greedy allocation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, algorithmic blueprint for implementing highly responsive, patient-benefiting adaptive trials, directly addressing the ethical imperative to minimize suboptimal care within studies. It will contribute a rigorous simulation framework for evaluating bandit algorithms in clinical contexts and offer practical insights for trialists in psychiatry and global health on balancing statistical learning with patient welfare.
proposals.novelty: While bandit algorithms are well-known in ML, their direct application as the core engine for a full clinical trial, integrated with contextual patient data and evaluated specifically for psychiatric treatments in resource-constrained settings, represents a novel synthesis of reinforcement learning theory and pragmatic trial methodology.

proposals.title: Expander-Flow Bayesian Adaptive Trials: A Graph-Based Framework for Resource-Constrained Clinical Learning
proposals.motivation: Bayesian adaptive clinical trials promise more efficient learning but often assume idealized, uniform patient enrollment and resource availability. Real-world trials face heterogeneous, time-varying constraints like site-specific recruitment limits and budget caps, creating a 'treatment gap' between statistical design and practical execution. This gap reduces the real-world impact of adaptive methods, particularly in global health settings with severe resource limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling a multi-arm trial as a capacitated flow network and applying expander-graph-inspired, Bayesian adaptive routing algorithms will yield a trial design that simultaneously optimizes statistical learning efficiency and respects complex operational constraints, outperforming standard adaptive designs under non-uniform resource landscapes.
proposals.method: We will formalize the trial as a graph: treatment arms and sites are nodes, edges are allocation pathways with capacities (e.g., max patients/site), and demands are statistical power requirements. A core Bayesian engine, using Thompson Sampling or a related bandit algorithm, will update treatment arm efficacy posteriors. This will guide a novel, online 'expander-flow' routing algorithm, dynamically adjusting patient flow to promising arms while ensuring the allocation network maintains high connectivity and respects all capacity constraints, akin to methods for maintaining expansion in dynamic graphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, algorithmic framework for integrating hard operational constraints into adaptive trial designs, moving beyond simulation-based heuristics. It will deliver a concrete, open-source design tool for trialists, directly addressing the 'practical executability' challenge highlighted in recent health systems research.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of expander graph flow techniques with Bayesian adaptive design is novel, creating a formal analog between network throughput optimization and constrained statistical learning. This goes beyond typical constraint-handling in trials, which is often ad-hoc, by providing a rigorous algorithmic foundation.

proposals.title: Adaptive Bayesian Learning for Rapidly Optimized Antiviral Dosing in Emerging Outbreaks
proposals.motivation: The initial response to novel infectious disease outbreaks, such as COVID-19, is hampered by a critical knowledge gap in optimal drug dosing. Traditional fixed-dose trials waste precious time and patient data, leading to subtherapeutic or toxic exposures. An adaptive system that learns from early patients to rationally dose subsequent ones could dramatically improve therapeutic outcomes from the earliest stages of an epidemic.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) framework, initialized with mechanism-based priors and updated via prospectively collected patient data, can converge to safe, effective, and personalized dosing regimens faster than standard clinical development paradigms during an outbreak.
proposals.method: We will develop a platform using hierarchical Bayesian PK/PD models with priors informed by pathogen class (e.g., protease inhibitor) and drug mechanism. The system will employ adaptive, patient-wise Bayesian updating via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo or variational inference. Dosing recommendations for each new patient cohort will be generated by optimizing a utility function (efficacy vs. safety) using the updated posterior, creating a closed-loop 'learning while treating' system. This integrates real-time viral load and safety biomarker data from a decentralized study network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, data-driven methodology for conducting 'learning clinical trials' in outbreak settings. It will yield a practical software platform for adaptive dose-finding and demonstrate via simulation studies how such a system could have accelerated optimal dosing in historical outbreaks, setting a new standard for agile therapeutic response.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian adaptive trials exist, this approach uniquely integrates mechanism-based priors with continuous, fine-grained model updating from rich PK/biomarker data at the individual patient level, framing outbreak response as an online machine learning problem for clinical decision-making.

proposals.title: A Convex Optimization Framework for Real-Time, High-Dimensional Personalization of Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Models
proposals.motivation: Personalized dosing in complex therapies like HIV is hindered by the computational intractability of high-dimensional PK/PD models with many patient covariates and uncertainty parameters. Static guidelines fail to adapt to individual patient dynamics, leading to suboptimal efficacy, toxicity, and resistance. This gap necessitates a framework for real-time, robust regimen optimization that is both mathematically rigorous and clinically feasible.
proposals.hypothesis: The problem of identifying optimal, patient-specific dose and duration schedules can be formulated as a large-scale convex optimization program (e.g., Semidefinite or Second-Order Cone Program), enabling efficient, real-time computation of robust regimens that were previously infeasible.
proposals.method: We will formulate multi-compartment PK/PD models with covariate-driven parameters and uncertainty sets as convex constraints. Using matrix completion techniques to handle sparse data, we will cast the dynamic optimization of efficacy, toxicity, and resistance preemption as a structured SDP/SOCP. The core technical innovation is the integration of fast interior-point solvers (e.g., SDPT3, MOSEK) with an adaptive constraint-generation scheme, allowing the model structure to update in real-time based on incoming patient data streams.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for high-dimensional therapeutic personalization, moving beyond simulation-based approaches to guaranteed-optimal, real-time dosing. It will deliver a proof-of-concept algorithm and open-source solver interface for HIV therapy, with principles generalizable to other chronic diseases. The research bridges theoretical optimization, matrix analysis, and translational clinical informatics.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in applying advanced convex optimization and matrix analysis, typically used in machine learning theory and control, to the core PK/PD parameter identification and control problem, enabling real-time adaptability previously deemed computationally impossible for high-dimensional models.

proposals.title: Adaptive Sensing for Just-in-Time Ecological Assessment: A Context-Aware Model for Minimizing Reactivity in Health Adherence Tracking
proposals.motivation: Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) is vital for tracking real-world health behaviors, but fixed or random prompting disrupts participants and causes measurement reactivity, reducing data validity. Passive smartphone sensing offers rich contextual data, but existing systems lack intelligent, adaptive triggering of surveys. This gap limits the scalability and effectiveness of digital interventions for chronic conditions and mental health, where adherence is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: A sensor-informed, adaptive EMA system that triggers prompts based on a probabilistic model of adherence context and model uncertainty will yield higher-quality self-report data with less participant burden and greater predictive validity for downstream adherence outcomes compared to standard time-based EMA.
proposals.method: We will develop a background smartphone agent that continuously processes passive sensor streams (GPS, accelerometer, app usage) using a Transformer-based sequence model to encode temporal context. This model will be pre-trained on anonymized behavioral data and then fine-tuned via federated learning on individual user data to preserve privacy. A decision module, formulated as a contextual multi-armed bandit, will use the model's inferred adherence probability and its own prediction uncertainty to trigger a brief, context-tailored EMA survey only during high-risk or high-uncertainty moments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for low-burden, high-fidelity adherence tracking in real-world settings. It will contribute a new dataset linking adaptive sensor-driven prompts to self-reported adherence and clinical outcomes. The findings will advance the design of Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (JITAIs) by formalizing the trade-off between inference confidence and solicited feedback.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior sensor-augmented EMA, our system actively uses model uncertainty as a core signal for adaptive sampling, framing prompt timing as an online learning problem. This integrates principles from efficient neural network training and algorithmic decision-making into behavioral health sensing.

proposals.title: Dynamic Hypergraph Partitioning of Ecological Momentary Assessment Data for Real-Time Prediction of Behavioral Adherence
proposals.motivation: Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via smartphones generates rich, multi-contextual data on health behaviors, but existing analysis methods often treat individuals in isolation or use static models, failing to capture the complex, time-varying interdependencies between behavioral contexts and social influences. This limits the ability to predict critical transitions, such as medication non-adherence in psychiatric care, which is a major contributor to the global treatment gap. A computational framework that can dynamically model these multi-faceted relationships is needed to enable timely, personalized interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling longitudinal EMA data as a dynamic, directed hypergraph and partitioning it using spectral and expander-based methods will reveal latent subgroups with cohesive multi-contextual behavioral patterns and allow for the detection of phase transitions that precede adherence breakdowns more accurately than static or individual time-series models.
proposals.method: We will construct a dynamic hypergraph where nodes are patients, and time-stamped hyperedges encode multi-dimensional contexts (location, social setting, affect) from EMA. Directionality will capture inferred temporal/causal influences. We will extend spectral hypergraph partitioning and multi-commodity flow algorithms (inspired by Arora et al.'s work on expander flows and graph partitioning) to this dynamic, directed setting. The model will be trained and fine-tuned on existing EMA datasets (e.g., from mental health studies) to optimize partition coherence over time and detect partition instability as a signal of behavioral phase transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework for dynamic, network-based behavioral phenotyping that fuses EMA with advanced graph algorithms. It will provide a new method for identifying at-risk patient subgroups and optimal intervention timing in behavioral health, directly addressing challenges in scaling personalized care. The resulting algorithms will also advance the theory of partitioning for dynamic, directed hypergraphs.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of EMA with dynamic hypergraph theory for real-time phenotyping is novel, as is the extension of geometric embedding and expander flow methods to model directed, temporal hyperedges representing multi-faceted behavioral contexts, moving beyond static correlation networks or simple vector embeddings.

proposals.title: Efficacy of Ultra-Short (4-6 Week) Digital Therapy Protocols: A Multi-Task Learning and Fine-Tuning Approach for Highly Selected Patient Cohorts
proposals.motivation: The global treatment gap for common mental health conditions remains vast, especially in low-resource settings. While scalable digital interventions show promise, their typical 8-12 week duration poses adherence challenges and limits reach. Developing evidence-based, ultra-short therapies could dramatically increase accessibility and reduce the treatment burden, aligning with the need for efficient, task-shifted care models demonstrated in global health research.
proposals.hypothesis: That a highly selective patient stratification protocol, combined with a multi-task learning architecture fine-tuned on ultra-short intervention sequences, can yield clinical outcomes for anxiety and mild depression that are non-inferior to standard-length digital cognitive behavioral therapy (dCBT) protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop a patient selection model using a transformer-based architecture trained on pre-therapy assessment data (symptoms, demographics, engagement predictors) to identify candidates for ultra-short therapy. For the intervention, we will adapt a proven dCBT model (e.g., Woebot-style architecture) by applying rigorous fine-tuning and curriculum learning on a dataset of therapy dialogues, training it to achieve core therapeutic functions within a compressed 4-6 week dialogue structure. Efficacy will be tested in a randomized controlled trial comparing the ultra-short protocol against a standard 8-week dCBT control in the selected cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for 'scaling down' therapy—making it not just more widespread but also more efficient—which is critical for public health impact. It will contribute a novel methodology for patient-therapy matching using modern ML and establish initial efficacy data for a new class of ultra-brief, digitally-delivered interventions.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on scaling up, this proposal inverts the paradigm to rigorously scale down treatment length. It uniquely combines patient selection algorithms from ML with the fine-tuning of therapeutic language models, moving beyond one-size-fits-all digital health solutions.

proposals.title: Generalizing Digital Mental Health Interventions: A Multi-Country Study on the Scalability and Fairness of Fine-Tuned Language Models for Community Health Workers
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Arora's work on ASHAs in South India, demonstrates the efficacy of task-shifting mental health care to community health workers (CHWs) using structured protocols. However, these interventions often rely on strict eligibility criteria and manual processes, limiting scalability. Scaling to diverse, real-world populations with varied linguistic, cultural, and clinical presentations is a critical unsolved challenge in global mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model, initially trained on high-quality clinical dialogue and then fine-tuned via parameter-efficient methods on data from a single region, can be effectively adapted to support CHWs in new global settings without significant performance degradation, provided adaptation uses locally generated, task-specific data.
proposals.method: We will develop a base transformer model (e.g., BERT or GPT-2 architecture) pre-trained on public clinical text and synthetic CHW-patient dialogues. This model will be initially fine-tuned on annotated data from the South India ASHA study. For scale-up, we will partner with CHW networks in 3-4 diverse countries (e.g., Brazil, Kenya, Vietnam). Using a cloud-based platform, CHWs will record de-identified interaction summaries, which will be used for further fine-tuning (via methods like adapter layers or prompt tuning) of specific model modules. We will evaluate generalization by measuring the model's accuracy in suggesting protocol steps and its fairness across demographic subgroups within each new population.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for the responsible, data-efficient scaling of AI-assisted mental health support tools across heterogeneous populations. It will yield insights into the transferability of clinical NLP models and produce a novel, multi-lingual dataset of real-world CHW interactions, benchmarking progress towards reducing the global treatment gap.
proposals.novelty: While prior work applies NLP to standardized clinical settings, this proposal uniquely focuses on generalization for non-specialist, community-based care across global contexts, explicitly testing whether local SGD-style fine-tuning methods from machine learning theory can improve model fairness and robustness in this highly variable domain.

proposals.title: Predicting Sustained Virologic Response Failure in Hepatitis C Treatment: A Machine Learning Framework for Relapse Risk from Baseline Patient Factors
proposals.motivation: Despite the high efficacy of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) for Hepatitis C, a subset of patients fails to achieve sustained virologic response (SVR), with relapse being a primary cause. Identifying these high-risk patients at baseline is crucial for optimizing treatment protocols, enabling targeted monitoring, and improving resource allocation in public health systems, especially in resource-limited settings like rural India. This addresses a critical treatment gap in global viral hepatitis elimination efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: That a machine learning model, trained exclusively on pre-treatment (baseline) clinical, demographic, and socio-economic data, can accurately predict patients who will not achieve SVR due to relapse, and that the model's learned representations will reveal interpretable, non-linear interactions between known risk factors.
proposals.method: We will leverage a longitudinal dataset from a South Indian cohort, including patients treated for HCV. We will frame relapse prediction as a binary classification task. Using methods from Arora's recent work, we will train a Transformer-based encoder on the structured baseline data, treating patient features as a sequence. We will employ careful fine-tuning strategies, inspired by analysis of generalization in local SGD, to prevent overfitting on limited medical data. Model interpretability will be achieved via attention weight analysis and integrated gradients to identify key predictive features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically actionable tool for pre-treatment risk stratification in HCV care. It will contribute to the methodological literature by demonstrating how modern sequence models (Transformers) can be effectively adapted and regularized for structured, small-sample medical prediction tasks, moving beyond traditional logistic regression. The findings could inform the design of future intervention studies targeting high-risk groups.
proposals.novelty: While baseline prediction of SVR is studied, applying a Transformer architecture to this structured medical inverse problem and rigorously analyzing its generalization in a low-data regime, drawing from recent theoretical insights in optimization, is novel. The approach moves beyond simple feature importance to model complex interactions mirroring clinical intuition.

proposals.title: Dual-Objective Optimization for Mental Healthcare: A Cost-Effectiveness Framework Using Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: The global treatment gap for mental health disorders remains vast, particularly in low-resource settings. While task-shifting to community health workers (like ASHAs in India) is a promising, cost-effective strategy, optimizing their intervention protocols to simultaneously maximize patient outcomes (e.g., symptom remission, SVR) and minimize systemic cost is an unsolved, high-stakes problem. Current healthcare optimization often treats cost and efficacy as separate constraints rather than a unified, adaptive objective.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-objective reinforcement learning framework, trained on real-world patient trajectory data, can discover adaptive treatment policies that Pareto-dominate standard fixed protocols, achieving superior cost-effectiveness by dynamically allocating resources and tailoring community health worker actions based on individual patient response and system state.
proposals.method: We will model the treatment process as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). The state includes patient health metrics, treatment history, and local resource levels; actions are ASHA-delivered interventions; rewards are a dual vector combining a clinical efficacy signal (e.g., PHQ-9 reduction proxy for SVR) and a negative cost signal. We will employ a multi-agent RL architecture where a central 'planner' agent (a Transformer-based policy network) learns to allocate tasks and resources to a population of 'worker' agents (modeling ASHAs), using a modified Q-learning algorithm with a Pareto-frontier optimization objective to handle the dual reward. Training will use historical data from studies like the ASHA psychiatric care trial, with offline RL and careful policy fine-tuning for safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for designing adaptive, cost-effective public health interventions. It will yield concrete, interpretable treatment policies that can be tested in field trials, advancing the field of AI for global health. Methodologically, it contributes a new approach to multi-objective optimization in sequential decision-making under real-world data constraints.
proposals.novelty: The integration of multi-agent RL for community health worker coordination with a formal Pareto-optimality search for cost-clinical outcome trade-offs is novel. Prior healthcare RL work typically focuses on single-patient clinical outcomes, not system-wide cost-effectiveness with human-in-the-loop task-shifting agents.

proposals.title: Finite-Sample Stability and Low-Dimensional Guarantees for SVR12 as a Universal Cure Endpoint in Heterogeneous Populations
proposals.motivation: Sustained virologic response 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12) is the accepted surrogate endpoint for hepatitis C cure, but its universal validity across diverse, real-world patient subpopulations lacks a rigorous, distribution-free statistical foundation. Current justifications often rely on high-dimensional asymptotic theory or empirical cohort studies, which may not guarantee robustness in sparse, heterogeneous, or non-stationary data environments common in global health. A definitive mathematical framework is needed to ensure treatment guidelines and regulatory decisions are equally reliable for all patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between SVR12 and long-term cure can be modeled via a low-dimensional, stable statistical manifold, and that finite-sample generalization guarantees for this relationship can be proven without relying on high-dimensional concentration of measure, thus making the endpoint universally valid across subpopulations.
proposals.method: We will adapt techniques from algorithmic stability and robust statistics, inspired by analyses of SGD generalization. The core approach is to treat the mapping from patient features (e.g., genotype, comorbidities, treatment history) to the binary outcome (SVR12 -> long-term cure) as a low-complexity function class. We will employ stability arguments—similar to those used to analyze Local SGD—to derive uniform convergence bounds that are independent of the ambient data dimension. This involves constructing interpretable, regularized models (e.g., sparse logistic or rule-based) whose predictions are provably stable under resampling of heterogeneous subpopulations, validated on real-world HCV patient data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first finite-sample, distribution-agnostic statistical proof for SVR12 as a universal cure endpoint, directly addressing concerns about its validity in underrepresented groups. It will deliver a new methodological toolkit for certifying surrogate endpoints in medicine with robust, interpretable guarantees. The results will strengthen the evidence base for simplified HCV monitoring and support similar validation efforts for surrogate endpoints in other infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in replacing asymptotic, high-dimensional statistical arguments with finite-sample, low-dimensional stability guarantees, explicitly designed for the heterogeneous data geometry of real-world health cohorts—a fusion of theoretical computer science rigor with pragmatic clinical epidemiology.

proposals.title: Sparse Cuts for Dense Problems: An Algorithmic Framework for Personalized Treatment Optimization in Advanced Cirrhosis
proposals.motivation: Advanced cirrhosis management is complex, with treatment decisions balancing efficacy against the risk of adverse events like hepatic encephalopathy or renal failure. Current clinical guidelines offer population-level strategies but lack personalization for heterogeneous patient states. Pre-2026, machine learning in healthcare has focused on prediction; there is a critical gap in using algorithmic optimization to actively design treatment sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal personalized treatment pathway for a cirrhotic patient can be framed as a sparsest cut problem on a structured graph of clinical states and interventions, and novel approximation algorithms for this domain-specific graph can outperform generic optimization methods in generating clinically plausible, high-efficacy regimens.
proposals.method: We will construct a bipartite graph connecting patient state vectors (lab values, symptoms) to treatment actions and subsequent outcome states. Edge weights will encode transition probabilities and costs derived from real-world data (e.g., EHRs). We will adapt and extend spectral partitioning and ARV-based sparsest cut algorithms, leveraging the inherent structure (e.g., temporal progression, known physiological constraints) to design a domain-specific approximation algorithm. The resulting sparse cuts will define treatment protocols, which will be evaluated via simulation and prepared for prospective validation in a pilot study.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel optimization-based framework for clinical decision sequence design, moving beyond predictive modeling, 2) a new graph partitioning algorithm tailored for structured biomedical data with potential theoretical improvements, and 3) a set of algorithmically-generated, personalized treatment protocols for advanced cirrhosis ready for clinical assessment.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of advanced theoretical computer science (graph partitioning) with granular treatment optimization for a complex chronic disease is novel. It directly applies Arora's expertise in approximation algorithms and algorithmic foundations of machine learning to a concrete, high-stakes health optimization problem.

proposals.title: Certifying Therapeutic Regimens in Cirrhosis via Multi-Commodity Flow: A Computational Framework for Patient-Specific Efficacy and Toxicity Prediction
proposals.motivation: Advanced cirrhosis drastically alters hepatic circulation and metabolism, creating a high-risk, heterogeneous patient population where standard therapeutic dosing often fails or causes toxicity. Current population-level pharmacokinetic models lack the granularity to predict individual outcomes in this complex, flow-constrained environment. A rigorous, patient-specific predictive tool is urgently needed to de-risk clinical trials and personalize treatment for this vulnerable group.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the cirrhotic liver as a constrained multi-commodity flow network, we can computationally certify whether a proposed drug regimen can be feasibly delivered to target tissues without violating patient-specific metabolic and hemodynamic constraints, thereby predicting its clinical efficacy and toxicity risk prior to administration.
proposals.method: We will construct a patient-specific graph model from clinical imaging (CT/MRI) and hemodynamic data, where nodes represent vascular junctions and tissue compartments, and edges have capacities derived from flow measurements and disease severity scores. Drugs and endogenous metabolites are modeled as distinct commodities with source-sink demands and edge consumption/production rules. We will apply and adapt multi-commodity flow theorems and polynomial-time approximation schemes (e.g., using multiplicative weight update methods) to solve the feasibility problem. The model will be tuned and validated against retrospective patient data on drug levels and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational certification tool for therapeutic planning in complex chronic liver disease, moving beyond statistical correlation to mechanistic, constraint-based prediction. It will establish a formal bridge between theoretical computer science (network flows) and translational hepatology, offering a new paradigm for patient-specific treatment validation.
proposals.novelty: While network models exist in systems biology, applying rigorous multi-commodity flow theory to certify therapeutic delivery in a pathologically altered organ is novel. The fusion of discrete optimization with granular clinical parameters to produce a pre-treatment 'safety/efficacy certificate' represents a significant departure from current pharmacokinetic modeling.

proposals.title: Spectral Clustering of Patient Graphs to Predict Optimal Duration for Ribavirin-Free Hepatitis C Therapy
proposals.motivation: Despite high cure rates with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs), the optimal treatment duration for ribavirin-free regimens remains debated, with standard 12-week courses failing for some patients. Current clinical trial analysis relies on coarse subgroup comparisons, missing complex, high-dimensional interactions between viral, host, and disease factors. A more nuanced, data-driven framework is needed to personalize therapy duration and improve resource allocation in global elimination efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients can be stratified into distinct, mechanistically meaningful subpopulations via spectral analysis of a similarity graph constructed from multi-parameter clinical data, and these subpopulations will exhibit systematically different probabilities of sustained virologic response (SVR) to short (e.g., 8-12 week) versus extended (e.g., 16-24 week) ribavirin-free DAA therapy.
proposals.method: We will construct a patient similarity graph from a clinical trial dataset, with nodes representing patients and weighted edges based on a multi-kernel similarity function integrating viral kinetics, IL28B genotype, liver fibrosis score, and baseline viral load. We will apply spectral clustering algorithms to partition this graph, analyzing the resulting clusters' expansion properties (conductance) and their correlation with SVR rates across different treatment durations. The analysis will be validated by training a predictive model (e.g., a fine-tuned transformer or graph neural network) on the spectral embeddings to forecast individual patient outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, topology-informed framework for clinical trial analysis that moves beyond average treatment effects. It will deliver a practical tool for stratifying patients by their inherent 'curability' network, potentially enabling shorter, effective therapy for many while identifying those needing extended courses. The method bridges theoretical computer science (spectral graph theory) and pragmatic clinical study design.
proposals.novelty: While graph methods exist in genomics, applying spectral partitioning and expansion analysis to model the *structure of treatment response* in a clinical cohort is novel. This approach frames differential treatment efficacy not as a simple covariate effect but as an emergent property of the patient population's relational topology.

proposals.title: Mechanism-Aware Retreatment Planning via Stratified Graph Embedding into Expander Protocol Networks
proposals.motivation: In mental health care, a significant proportion of patients do not respond to initial therapy, leading to a 'treatment gap.' Current retreatment strategies often oscillate between uniform protocols and an intractable number of personalized sequences, lacking a principled, robust framework. This work addresses the critical need for systematic, data-driven retreatment planning for non-responders, grounded in their shared latent failure mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling a cohort of prior non-responders as a heterogeneous graph stratified by latent failure mechanisms, and embedding this graph into a minimal, robust 'expander' network of treatment protocols, will yield a retreatment strategy that is more efficient and generalizable than uniform approaches, while being more scalable than fully personalized planning.
proposals.method: We will construct a patient graph using pre-2026 methods: nodes represent patients with features from their health records and initial treatment study data; edges are weighted based on similarity in high-dimensional 'failure embeddings' derived from a fine-tuned transformer model analyzing patient trajectories and outcomes. We will then formalize the search for an optimal 'expander' protocol network—a sparse, highly connected graph of treatment options—as a combinatorial optimization problem. The core technical task is to compute an embedding of the patient graph into this protocol network with bounded congestion, using techniques inspired by spectral graph theory and multi-commodity flow approximations, to identify stratified, adaptive retreatment pathways.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel computational framework for mechanism-aware retreatment planning in psychiatry, directly addressing the treatment gap. It contributes a formal graph-theoretic model for patient stratification beyond simple clustering and introduces the concept of 'expander protocol networks' as a robust design principle for adaptive treatment systems.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of expander graph theory from theoretical computer science with clinical retreatment planning is novel. Framing the optimal retreatment design as a bounded-congestion graph embedding problem, where the target network's expansion properties guarantee systemic robustness, is a non-obvious and principled advance over existing methods.

proposals.title: A Graph-Theoretic Stress Test for SVR12: Algorithmic Identification of High-Conductance Subpopulations to Validate a Universal Cure Endpoint in Hepatitis C
proposals.motivation: Sustained virologic response at 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12) is the accepted surrogate endpoint for hepatitis C cure, but its universal reliability across diverse patient populations remains a critical, unresolved question in clinical hepatology. Current validation relies on ad-hoc subgroup analyses, which may miss complex, multi-factorial subpopulations where late relapse risk is concentrated. A principled, computational method to systematically search for potential failure modes is needed to strengthen the evidence base for this cornerstone of treatment policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the cured patient population as a similarity graph and algorithmically identifying the subgraph with maximum conductance—a measure of strong internal connectivity but weak external links—we can isolate patient subgroups where the SVR12-to-long-term-cure transition is most internally consistent yet most distinct from the broader population, thereby providing a rigorous stress test for SVR12's universal validity.
proposals.method: We will construct a patient graph from a large, multi-center cohort of patients achieving SVR12, with nodes representing patients and edges weighted by similarity across features including HCV genotype, treatment regimen, baseline viral load, fibrosis stage, comorbidities (e.g., HIV), and demographic factors. We will employ spectral clustering and local graph partitioning algorithms, specifically targeting the computational objective of finding a subgraph with maximum conductance. This identified high-conductance subpopulation will then be linked to long-term virologic outcomes (e.g., 5-year follow-up data) to empirically test the stability of SVR12 within it.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, algorithmic framework for validating surrogate endpoints in medicine, moving beyond traditional epidemiology. It will deliver a concrete, data-driven assessment of whether SVR12 holds even in the most 'conductive'—and thus potentially most biologically coherent—subpopulations, offering either strengthened confidence or revealing precise failure modes. The methodology can be generalized to other diseases where long-term outcomes are predicted by short-term surrogates.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of rigorous theoretical computer science (graph conductance optimization) with a high-stakes clinical validation problem, translating an abstract algorithmic objective into a concrete tool for stress-testing medical dogma. This approach is non-obvious as it re-frames subgroup analysis as an optimization problem rather than a pre-specified categorical exercise.

proposals.title: Robust Multiplicative Weights Updates with Approximate Oracles: Theory and Applications in Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: The Multiplicative Weights Update (MWU) method is a cornerstone of online learning and optimization, but its classical analysis assumes access to a perfect linear optimization oracle. This is unrealistic in modern large-scale applications, such as fine-tuning large language models or training on decentralized patient health data, where gradient computations are noisy, approximate, or constrained by computational budgets. A rigorous framework for MWU with imperfect oracles would bridge theory and practice, enabling more robust and efficient algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MWU can be made robust to approximate, noisy, or computationally limited oracles while retaining sublinear regret, and that such robust variants can provide concrete benefits in applications like federated fine-tuning and constrained clinical study optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework categorizing oracle imperfections (additive noise, multiplicative approximation, query complexity limits). We will analyze regret bounds for MWU variants under these conditions, drawing connections to local SGD and implicit regularization. The theory will be validated empirically by applying the algorithms to two tasks: 1) federated fine-tuning of transformer language models with communication-limited clients, and 2) simulating a resource-constrained treatment allocation problem inspired by mental health intervention studies, where the oracle solves a simplified linear program.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive theory of MWU with general imperfect oracles, extending its applicability to real-world constrained settings. It will deliver practical, theoretically grounded algorithms for distributed fine-tuning and resource allocation, directly contributing to more efficient machine learning training and equitable health intervention planning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied specific stochastic oracles, a unified treatment of computationally bounded, approximate, and noisy oracles for MWU is lacking. The explicit connection to modern fine-tuning paradigms and health informatics models, informed by the researcher's recent work, provides a novel and impactful application domain.

proposals.title: Unifying Optimization and Inference: Deriving Modern Fine-Tuning and Local SGD Algorithms from the Multiplicative Weights Update Framework
proposals.motivation: The Multiplicative Weights Update (MWU) is a powerful meta-algorithm known to unify diverse algorithms from game theory to online learning. Recent work by Arora and others has explored its connections to gradient descent and boosting. However, its potential to provide a unified perspective on contemporary machine learning techniques, particularly those used in fine-tuning large language models and distributed training, remains underexplored. A systematic investigation could yield new insights into algorithm design, generalization, and convergence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key algorithms for modern neural network training and inference—specifically, adaptive fine-tuning methods like AdamW and communication-efficient distributed methods like Local SGD—can be formally derived as instantiations of the MWU meta-framework under specific assumptions and loss constructions.
proposals.method: We will first construct a general loss minimization setup where the learner's updates correspond to an MWU player in a repeated game against a loss-selecting adversary. We will then derive specific algorithmic families: (1) For fine-tuning, we will show how adaptive gradient methods emerge from MWU with a carefully chosen potential function and step-size schedule linked to gradient history. (2) For distributed training, we will model local steps as MWU updates on local data and communication rounds as a consensus step within the MWU framework, analyzing how this perspective explains generalization benefits. The analysis will leverage tools from regret minimization and online convex optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the theoretical umbrella of MWU, providing a novel, unified lens through which to understand and design optimization algorithms. It will offer new theoretical explanations for the empirical success of methods like AdamW and Local SGD, potentially leading to more robust and communication-efficient training schemes. The framework may also suggest new hybrid algorithms for federated learning and adaptive fine-tuning in healthcare NLP applications.
proposals.novelty: While MWU is known to underlie classical algorithms, its direct connection to modern deep learning optimizers and distributed training protocols is non-obvious and largely unexplored. The novelty lies in formally bridging this gap and using the connection to derive new theoretical guarantees for these practical methods.

ideas.title: Proactive Retention and Bias-Mitigated Assessment of SVR Durability and Long-Term Outcomes
ideas.description: This study assesses the durability of sustained virologic response (SVR) and long-term clinical outcomes in all patients who initiate antiviral therapy, with a primary focus on preventing and analytically correcting for attrition bias. A comprehensive, patient-centered retention system using navigators, technology, and flexible visits is deployed to maximize follow-up across all patient subgroups. Advanced statistical methods are pre-specified to handle missing data and provide unbiased estimates of SVR durability, hepatocellular carcinoma incidence, and mortality for the entire real-world treatment cohort.

ideas.title: Adapting Double-Blind Auditions to Mitigate Open-Label Bias in Clinical Trials
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that open-label trial design significantly biases patient-reported outcomes and adherence. Drawing an analogy from orchestral auditions, where the solution to evaluator bias was the physical screen of a blind audition, we adapt this core principle to clinical research. The adapted idea proposes implementing a 'blinded outcome adjudication' layer within open-label trials: key efficacy data (e.g., electronic patient diaries, sensor-based adherence metrics) are collected and systematically stripped of treatment-arm identifiers before being presented to the independent endpoint adjudication committee. This procedural intervention aims to isolate the assessment of the treatment's true effect from the psychological and expectancy biases inherent in the open-label patient-provider interaction, thereby providing a more objective measure of efficacy even within a non-blinded study design.

ideas.title: Graph-Theoretic Partitioning of Patient Cohorts to Optimize and Personalize HCV Treatment Regimen Evaluation
ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced hypergraph and directed graph partitioning algorithms to the clinical evaluation of ledipasvir-sofosbuvir in treatment-naive HCV patients. The patient population, along with their multi-factorial clinical data (viral genotype, comorbidities, biomarkers, social determinants), is modeled as a hypergraph, where hyperedges connect patients sharing multiple overlapping characteristics. Using expander flow and geometric embedding techniques, the analysis identifies the most computationally 'hard' patient clusters to separate from the overall cohort. These tightly interconnected, difficult-to-isolate subgroups represent critical subpopulations where treatment efficacy is most ambiguous, guiding the targeted enrollment or stratified analysis within the trial to maximize information gain about personalized treatment response.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Sparsity Optimization for Personalized Treatment Regimens in Advanced Cirrhosis
ideas.description: This research applies advanced graph partitioning and sparsest cut algorithms to the problem of optimizing treatment regimens for patients with advanced cirrhosis. The core idea is to model the patient's clinical state (symptoms, lab values, comorbidities) and potential treatment decisions as a high-dimensional graph, where finding the optimal treatment pathway is analogous to finding a sparse cut that separates a 'beneficial outcome' cluster from an 'adverse event' cluster with minimal intervention 'cost'. By developing approximation algorithms that go beyond the O(√log n) barrier for this specific, structured biomedical domain, the project aims to generate computationally discovered, personalized treatment protocols. These protocols can then be prospectively assessed in the target patient population, creating a closed-loop system where algorithmic discovery directly feeds into clinical trial design for validation.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Sensor-Informed Ecological Momentary Assessment for Adherence Tracking
ideas.description: This system combines passive smartphone sensing with intelligent, just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) to track health behavior adherence. A background model continuously analyzes streams of passive sensor data (location, activity, app usage) to infer context and the likelihood of adherence behavior. Instead of fixed-interval prompts, the system triggers brief, context-relevant EMA surveys only when the model detects a high-risk situation for non-adherence or when behavioral inference is uncertain. This approach minimizes participant disruption, reduces measurement reactivity, and increases the ecological validity and predictive power of the collected data.

ideas.title: Digital Phenotype-Informed Adaptive Trials for Mental Health using Constrained Multi-Armed Bandits
ideas.description: This system overcomes the endpoint mismatch of traditional adaptive designs by grounding the adaptation logic in a continuous, multi-dimensional digital phenotype derived from smartphone sensors and EMAs. A Bayesian model translates this high-frequency data into a personalized composite utility score reflecting core symptoms, functioning, and side effects. A constrained multi-armed bandit algorithm uses this dynamic score to allocate patients to optimal intervention components in real-time, while hard safety rules prevent harmful allocations. The design thus enables truly patient-centered, context-aware adaptation for complex mental health interventions.

ideas.title: Apply Multi-Armed Bandit Algorithms from Machine Learning to Clinical Trial Patient Allocation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the multi-armed bandit problem from machine learning to the context of clinical trial methodology. The analogous field of reinforcement learning uses algorithms like Thompson Sampling to dynamically manage the exploration-exploitation trade-off in uncertain environments, such as online advertising or recommendation systems. The adapted research idea applies this algorithmic framework as the core decision engine for a Bayesian adaptive trial. By modeling treatment arms as 'bandits' and patient outcomes as 'rewards,' the trial can use real-time posterior sampling to allocate each new patient to a regimen, continuously optimizing allocation towards the most effective options while efficiently learning about all candidates. This creates a highly responsive, patient-centric, and statistically efficient trial design that maximizes overall therapeutic benefit for participants within the study.

ideas.title: Adapt continuous quality improvement from manufacturing
ideas.description: Implement a Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle framework at ECHO sites to iteratively improve treatment processes.

ideas.title: Dynamic Hypergraph Partitioning for Real-Time Behavioral Phenotyping
ideas.description: This research fuses ecological momentary assessment (EMA) with advanced hypergraph partitioning to create a novel computational framework for behavioral science. Smartphone-based real-time adherence data is modeled as a dynamic, directed hypergraph, where nodes are individuals, hyperedges represent multi-faceted behavioral contexts (e.g., time, location, social setting, reported affect), and edge directions capture temporal and causal influences. By extending geometric embedding and expander flow methods to this structure, the approach can partition the hypergraph to identify cohesive subgroups of individuals with similar multi-contextual adherence patterns and, crucially, detect when these patterns undergo critical phase transitions indicative of adherence breakdown or intervention success. This enables a new form of dynamic, network-based phenotyping that predicts individual risk and optimal intervention timing far more precisely than static models or simple time-series tracking.

ideas.title: Long-term follow-up for sustained virologic response
ideas.description: Assess durability of SVR and long-term clinical outcomes post-treatment.

ideas.title: Spectral Network Analysis of HCV Treatment Response to Optimize Ribavirin-Free Therapy Duration
ideas.description: This research synthesizes spectral graph theory with clinical trial design for hepatitis C therapy. It models a patient cohort's multi-parameter data (viral kinetics, host genetics, liver fibrosis) as a high-dimensional graph, where patients are nodes and similarities in response profiles are edges. By applying spectral partitioning and analyzing the graph's expansion properties, the method identifies distinct subpopulations with inherently different 'conductances' to cure—revealing why a 12-week duration suffices for one spectral cluster while another requires 24 weeks. This data-driven, network-based stratification moves beyond simple cohort comparisons to a mechanistic, topology-informed framework for personalizing ribavirin-free direct-acting antiviral regimens.

ideas.title: Identifying Subpopulations with Maximum 'Virologic Connectivity' to Validate SVR12 as a Universal Cure Endpoint
ideas.description: This research applies the computational objective of finding a subgraph with maximum conductance to the clinical question of SVR12 reliability. We model the population of cured patients as a graph, where nodes are patients and edges are weighted by shared characteristics (e.g., genotype, treatment history, comorbidities). The goal is to identify the subpopulation (subgraph) with maximum 'virologic conductance'—meaning the subset where the probability of transitioning from SVR12 to long-term cure is most internally consistent yet most distinct from the broader population. By rigorously testing if SVR12 holds even in these maximally 'conductive' (i.e., internally well-connected but boundary-sparse) subpopulations, we provide a stress-test for its universal validity, moving beyond ad-hoc subgroup analysis to a principled, algorithmic search for potential failure modes.

ideas.title: Quantifying Open-Label Bias in Efficacy Trials via Pre-Treatment Minority Variant Dynamics
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel trial design for antiviral or antimicrobial studies where deep sequencing is used to detect and quantify pre-treatment minority resistant variants in all patients. In an open-label arm, patient-reported outcomes and adherence are tracked alongside the dynamics of these minority variants. The key innovation is using the differential expansion of these pre-existing resistant variants—a hard biological signal independent of patient perception—as an internal comparator to quantify how much of the overall clinical outcome (e.g., symptom improvement, viral load reduction) is attributable to the pharmacological effect versus the bias induced by the open-label design's knowledge of treatment assignment. This allows for the first objective calibration of expectation bias, separating it from true drug efficacy in a way self-reported measures alone cannot achieve.

ideas.title: Challenging the necessity of ribavirin by analogy to adjuvant chemotherapy de-escalation in oncology
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from oncology, where the necessity of toxic adjuvant chemotherapy was successfully challenged and de-escalated for patient subgroups defined by biomarkers after high-efficacy primary treatments became standard. The analogous solution involved risk-stratifying patients and running de-escalation trials. Adapted to HCV therapy, the research idea challenges the assumption that ribavirin is necessary for all patients achieving high SVR rates with DAAs. It proposes identifying a biomarker-defined 'low-risk' subgroup (e.g., based on IL28B genotype, low baseline viral load, or mild fibrosis) and conducting a clinical trial of a ribavirin-free DAA regimen in this population. The goal is to maintain the high cure rate while significantly improving the treatment's safety and tolerability profile.

ideas.title: Robust, Low-Dimensional Statistical Guarantees for SVR12 as a Universal Cure Endpoint
ideas.description: This research develops new statistical and analytical techniques to definitively prove SVR12 is a sufficient predictor of long-term virologic cure across all patient subpopulations. The core innovation is to avoid reliance on high-dimensional measure concentration phenomena, which can fail in sparse or heterogeneous real-world cohorts. Instead, it constructs robust, finite-sample guarantees using low-dimensional, interpretable models that are stable across the diverse data geometry of different subpopulations. This provides a foundational mathematical justification for SVR12 as a universal endpoint, independent of specific population distributions or asymptotic assumptions.

ideas.title: Bayesian Adaptive Trial Designs with Non-Uniform Patient Flow Constraints
ideas.description: This research synthesizes Bayesian adaptive trial designs with algorithms for handling non-uniform capacities in flow networks. The core idea is to model a multi-arm clinical trial as a weighted graph, where edges represent pathways for patient allocation with capacities reflecting real-world constraints like site enrollment limits or budget caps, and demands corresponding to statistical power needs. A Bayesian adaptive algorithm, inspired by expander flow methods, would dynamically re-route the 'flow' of patients to the most promising treatment arms while continuously respecting these heterogeneous operational constraints. This results in a novel trial design methodology that jointly optimizes for learning efficiency and practical executability under complex, time-varying resource landscapes.

ideas.title: Stratified Graph Embedding for Mechanism-Aware Retreatment Planning
ideas.description: This research direction models a cohort of prior non-responders to a therapy as a graph, where patients are nodes and edges represent shared latent failure mechanisms. The core problem is to find the smallest 'expander' protocol network—a robust, efficiently navigable treatment space—into which this heterogeneous graph can be embedded with bounded congestion. This embedding identifies a minimal set of adaptive retreatment pathways that respect the distinct failure strata while ensuring systemic robustness and efficiency, thereby providing a principled alternative to uniform retreatment or an intractable proliferation of highly personalized protocols.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Response-Guided Treatment Duration Platform Using Continuous Biomarker Monitoring
ideas.description: This research develops a clinical trial and treatment platform that dynamically determines optimal treatment duration for each patient, moving beyond predefined fixed intervals. The system uses frequent, protocolized monitoring of validated biomarkers (e.g., viral load, tumor size, symptom scores, pharmacodynamic markers) and patient-reported tolerability data. A pre-specified, algorithm-based decision rule, potentially powered by reinforcement learning, analyzes this continuous data stream to recommend treatment continuation or cessation in real time. The outcome is a personalized treatment duration curve and a generalizable, biomarker-defined stopping framework that maximizes efficacy and minimizes unnecessary exposure for heterogeneous patient populations.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Adaptive Retreatment Duration for Prior Non-Responders
ideas.description: This research proposes that for patients who have failed a prior therapy, the optimal retreatment strategy must be personalized along two axes: the therapeutic mechanism and the treatment duration. Instead of applying a standard 12- or 24-week retreat for all non-responders, the duration is tailored based on whether the prior failure was due to, for example, rapid viral rebound (suggesting a need for longer, more intensive duration) or a slow, suboptimal response (where a shorter, intensified regimen might suffice). This creates a mechanism-adaptive dosing schedule, where the biological driver of failure directly informs not just the drug choice, but the critical time-exposure needed to overcome it. The goal is to define distinct retreatment pathways—such as '8-week intensive' for one failure phenotype versus '16-week augmented' for another—to maximize efficacy and efficiency where previous fixed-duration approaches have failed.

ideas.title: Adaptive Bayesian PK/PD Platform for Outbreak Response
ideas.description: This approach creates a learning clinical trial and treatment system for novel infectious disease outbreaks. It uses a Bayesian pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling framework that is initialized with broad priors based on pathogen class or mechanism of action. The system then prospectively collects rich PK, biomarker (e.g., viral load), and safety data from the first treated patients, using this data to rapidly refine the model and optimize dosing regimens for subsequent patients. This enables progressively personalized and rational dosing from the earliest stages of an outbreak, bridging the critical gap until traditional characterization is complete.

ideas.title: Convex Optimization for Real-Time, High-Dimensional PK/PD Personalization
ideas.description: This research fuses PK/PD modeling from HIV therapy with advanced convex optimization and matrix analysis to create a novel framework for dynamic, high-dimensional therapeutic personalization. It formulates the problem of identifying optimal, patient-specific dose and duration schedules as a large-scale semidefinite program (SDP) or second-order cone program (SOCP), where the model structure and constraints adapt to individual patient data in real-time. By leveraging fast convex optimization solvers and matrix completion techniques, the framework can efficiently handle complex, multi-compartment models with numerous covariates and uncertainty parameters, which were previously computationally intractable. This allows for the rapid, clinically feasible calculation of robust, personalized regimens that simultaneously optimize efficacy, minimize toxicity, and preempt resistance, moving beyond static dosing guidelines.

ideas.title: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) for Treatment Non-Response
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all prior non-responders to a therapy require the same retreatment strategy. Drawing an analogy from Reliability Engineering, specifically the practice of Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), the solution involves moving from a uniform response to a stratified one based on failure root cause. In the analogous field, system failures are not fixed uniformly but are analyzed to identify specific failure mechanisms (e.g., material fatigue, design flaw) which then dictate the tailored corrective action. Adapted to clinical research, this translates to developing a diagnostic framework to classify the biological or physiological mechanism behind a patient's initial non-response to a therapy. The adapted research idea is to create a 'failure taxonomy' for treatment non-response (e.g., categories like inadequate drug exposure, target mutation, immune evasion, or protective microenvironment). Retreatment would then be precisely selected or designed to counteract the identified specific failure mode, fundamentally personalizing the approach to therapeutic resistance and potentially salvaging responses in stratified sub-populations of prior non-responders.

ideas.title: Multi-commodity flow modeling for optimizing therapeutic regimen delivery in advanced cirrhosis
ideas.description: This research applies theorems from multi-commodity flow theory to model the liver's circulatory and metabolic pathways as a constrained network. The model is used to computationally certify whether a proposed therapeutic regimen can be effectively and safely 'routed' to target tissues in patients with advanced cirrhosis, given disease-altered flow capacities and competing metabolic demands. This provides a rigorous, patient-specific predictive certificate of regimen efficacy and toxicity risk prior to clinical trial or administration, moving beyond population-level statistics. The approach synergistically uses network certification to strengthen clinical assessment, and clinical parameters to ground the abstract flow model in biological reality.

ideas.title: Optimizing HCV Treatment Regimens via Constraint-Relaxed Semidefinite Programming for Genotype-Specific Viral Kinetics
ideas.description: This research fuses the clinical evaluation of ledipasvir-sofosbuvir for HCV genotype 1b with the mathematical concept of relaxing triangle inequality constraints in semidefinite programming (SDP). The synergy lies in using constraint-relaxed SDP frameworks to model the complex, high-dimensional interactions within the viral-host-drug system, which are not fully captured by traditional pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic models. By treating patient virological and immunological parameters as variables in an SDP optimization, and relaxing classical constraints to better fit biological reality, we can identify novel, genotype-specific predictive signatures of treatment success or failure. This enables the data-driven design of optimized, potentially personalized treatment protocols that maximize sustained virologic response while minimizing cost and duration, a goal neither pure clinical trials nor pure theoretical optimization could achieve alone.

ideas.title: Assess drug regimen in advanced cirrhosis using ecological resilience modeling
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to assess a drug regimen in patients with advanced cirrhosis by applying an analogy from ecosystem ecology. In ecology, the resilience of a stressed system (like a coral reef) is evaluated using multi-parameter stability metrics and threshold modeling to predict regime shifts. The analogous solution involves monitoring interconnected indicators to see if an intervention helps the system absorb stress without collapsing. Adapted to hepatology, the study would investigate the efficacy and safety of a regimen by defining a 'hepatic resilience profile'—a composite of dynamic, frequently measured clinical, hemodynamic, inflammatory, and microbiomic variables. The core adapted idea is to model whether the treatment enhances the liver's functional network resilience, allowing it to withstand insults without crossing a critical threshold into irreversible decompensation, thereby offering a more nuanced safety and efficacy assessment in this high-risk population.

ideas.title: A Phase 2b, Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of [Specific Drug X] for the Prevention of Hepatic Decompensation in Patients with Child-Pugh B Cirrhosis

The primary efficacy endpoint is the time to first adjudicated hepatic decompensation event (ascites, variceal hemorrhage, hepatic encephalopathy). Key safety outcomes include serial monitoring for changes in renal function, serum sodium, and the incidence of treatment-emergent serious adverse events, with pre-specified rules for dose adjustment or discontinuation.

ideas.title: Dual Certification of Viral Reservoir Dynamics as an Expander Flow Problem
ideas.description: This research direction frames the long-term durability of a sustained virologic response (SVR) as a dual certification problem in combinatorial optimization. It models the patient's anatomical compartments (e.g., liver, lymphoid tissue) and immune cell trafficking as a network, where a latent viral reservoir constitutes a potential source of flow. The core question becomes whether one can construct an optimal 'expander flow'—a pattern of immune surveillance and compartment connectivity—that certifies the impossibility of viral rebound by demonstrating the network's robust expansion properties inherently suppresses viral spread. This approach moves beyond observational follow-up to provide a predictive, model-based certificate of cure, linking long-term clinical outcomes to fundamental graph-theoretic invariants of the host's biological network.

ideas.title: Explicit Concentration Scaffolding: A Parameterized Framework for High-Dimensional Analysis
ideas.description: This research program accepts measure concentration as an inescapable feature of high-dimensional spaces but seeks to move beyond generic, asymptotic concentration results (like Lévy's lemma) that often lead to overly pessimistic or one-size-fits-all guarantees. It develops a suite of techniques to explicitly bound and parameterize concentration rates for specific function classes and data distributions, creating a 'concentration scaffold' for analysis. This allows for the derivation of precise, non-asymptotic performance bounds that adapt to the problem's intrinsic dimensionality and structure, enabling more nuanced algorithm design. The outcome is a rigorous methodology that identifies when weak concentration can be leveraged for efficiency and when strong concentration must be mitigated for robustness.

ideas.title: Develop Robust Analysis via Finite-Sample Concentration Inequalities and Local Geometry
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the common assumption in high-dimensional statistics and machine learning that norms and distances concentrate tightly around their means—a phenomenon often used to simplify analysis. Drawing an analogy from statistical physics, where macroscopic laws fail for small systems and scientists instead use exact fluctuation theorems, we propose to develop analysis techniques that do not depend on high-dimensional measure concentration. The analogous solution from small-system thermodynamics is to move beyond the thermodynamic limit and characterize the full distribution of observables. Adapted to this context, the research will develop non-asymptotic, finite-sample concentration inequalities that are explicitly dependent on dimension and the local geometric structure (e.g., intrinsic dimensionality, curvature) of the data distribution. The goal is to create robust analytical tools that provide valid guarantees even in moderate dimensions or for distributions where concentration is slow, by directly bounding the probability mass outside typical sets instead of assuming it vanishes.

ideas.title: Adapting Quantum Error Correction's Concatenated Code Structure to Break Approximation Barriers
ideas.description: This research seed proposes using analogical reasoning from quantum error correction to attack the long-standing O(√log n) approximation barrier for Sparsest Cut. The analogous problem is protecting quantum information from noise, solved via concatenated codes which recursively encode data to exponentially suppress errors below a threshold. The adapted idea is to design a novel, hierarchical rounding framework for semi-definite programming (SDP) relaxations of Sparsest Cut. This framework would involve multiple levels of solution refinement, where each level acts on the output of the previous one to systematically improve the cut's quality. The goal is to prove that for a class of problem instances (analogous to physical error rates below threshold), this recursive process can achieve an approximation ratio that scales better than O(√log n), potentially as a constant or a lower-order polylogarithmic factor, by showing the 'error' in the cut quality decreases exponentially with the number of hierarchical levels.

ideas.title: Constructive Geometric Embeddings for High-Dimensional Viral Variant Analysis
ideas.description: This research direction applies the principles of constructive geometric embedding theorems to the high-dimensional data generated by viral deep sequencing. The goal is to develop explicit, algorithmic methods to project the vast mutational space of a viral population (e.g., HIV, HCV) into lower-dimensional geometric structures that are interpretable and actionable. This enables not just the detection of pre-treatment minority resistant variants, but also the constructive mapping of their relative frequencies, mutational pathways, and distances from dominant strains within a defined geometric model. The synergy yields a new analytical toolkit for virology that provides a rigorous, computable representation of viral fitness landscapes and drug resistance evolution.

ideas.title: Problem-Specific Cutting-Plane Discovery for SDP Tightening
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic method to discover and apply high-impact constraints for tightening semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations of specific combinatorial problems. Instead of a generic search, it starts from the linear programming (LP) relaxation polytope of a target problem (e.g., Max-Cut, Sparsest Cut) and algorithmically generates candidate cutting planes that are valid for the integer polytope but violated by the current SDP solution. An adversarial separation oracle ranks these constraints by their bound improvement per computational cost, integrating only the most effective ones into a hybrid SDP+LP cutting-plane solver. This ensures the new constraints are not only valid but demonstrably effective for practical optimization.

ideas.title: Using Metric Embeddings to Construct and Analyze Integrality Gaps for SDP Relaxations
ideas.description: This research direction uses low-distortion metric embedding techniques not to round an SDP solution, but to construct hard instances that witness the integrality gap of the SDP relaxation with triangle inequalities. By embedding a hard metric space (where the optimization problem is difficult) into the SDP solution space with controlled distortion, one can generate candidate gap instances. Analyzing the SDP value versus the true integral value on these constructed instances then yields lower bounds on the integrality gap. This approach provides a powerful, geometry-driven framework for establishing the ultimate limitations of SDP relaxations, moving beyond existential gap proofs to constructive ones rooted in metric space theory.

ideas.title: Apply Rare-Cell Sequencing from Cancer Diagnostics to Detect Pre-Treatment Minority Resistant Variants
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cancer genomics—specifically, the use of error-corrected, ultra-deep sequencing to detect rare, pre-existing treatment-resistant clones in liquid biopsies—to the problem of detecting pre-treatment minority resistant variants in virology. In cancer, techniques like unique molecular identifier (UMI)-based sequencing of cell-free DNA are used to identify low-frequency somatic mutations that confer therapy resistance. By applying this methodological framework to viral deep sequencing, we can develop a highly sensitive assay to quantify drug-resistant viral variants (e.g., in HIV, HCV, or influenza) present at frequencies below 1% in a patient's baseline sample. This adapted approach would provide a powerful clinical tool to predict treatment failure risk and personalize antiviral therapy regimens from the outset, moving beyond the detection limits of standard population sequencing.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Scale Spectral Partitioning via Regularized Flow Diffusion
ideas.description: This research develops a computationally tractable algorithm that unifies spectral graph theory with a concrete, regularized flow diffusion process to achieve robust graph partitioning. The core innovation is a防御性的 diffusion procedure, regularized via higher-order eigenvectors and local conductance constraints, which iteratively refines cut boundaries to be stable against edge perturbations and outliers. The theoretical analysis provides new, computable bounds that adapt the Cheeger inequality to a noisy, adversarial setting. The outcome is a practical system with strong empirical performance on real-world networks and provable robustness guarantees, moving the original theoretical insight into an applicable algorithmic framework.
