ideas.title: Metric Embedding-Guided Discovery of SDP Constraints
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how the theory of low-distortion metric embeddings can guide the discovery of novel, valid constraints for semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations of combinatorial problems. By analyzing which geometric properties (beyond triangle inequality) are preserved or distorted under specific embeddings like Bourgain or Fréchet, one can derive necessary conditions that the SDP solution vectors must satisfy. This provides a principled, theoretical framework for generating constraint families that tighten the relaxation more effectively. The approach also enables new rounding algorithms that leverage the structure of these embedding-informed constraints to achieve better approximation guarantees.

ideas.title: Scalable SDPs via Structure-Aware First-Order Methods with Low-Rank Factorization
ideas.description: This research develops a scalable framework for semidefinite programming by synergistically combining low-rank factorization of the matrix variable with structure-exploiting first-order optimization algorithms. It explicitly addresses the memory and per-iteration cost bottlenecks by maintaining an implicit factored representation and utilizing chordal sparsity patterns to decompose large, dense constraints. The approach incorporates randomized linear algebra for efficient approximate eigenvalue computations and adaptive rank management. This results in a practical solver capable of handling large-scale SDPs arising in machine learning, control, and combinatorial optimization that are beyond the reach of standard interior-point methods.

ideas.title: Applying Cartographic Map Projection Techniques to SDP Rounding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of cartographic map projections to the task of rounding Semidefinite Programming (SDP) solutions. In cartography, the spherical Earth is embedded onto a 2D map using a projection chosen to preserve specific features (e.g., Mercator for angles, equal-area for sizes) with acceptable distortion elsewhere. Analogously, the high-dimensional SDP solution, which often represents points on a hypersphere or in a positive semidefinite cone, must be embedded into a simpler discrete space. The adapted research idea is to use low-distortion metric embedding techniques not as a generic tool, but as a tailored 'projection' designed by analyzing the SDP solution's geometry. The goal is to preserve the pairwise relationships (distances or angles) that are most critical for satisfying the original problem's constraints, thereby creating a biased embedding that yields a better approximation guarantee when used for randomized rounding. This is the computational equivalent of choosing the optimal map projection for a specific geographic task.

ideas.title: Apply Expander Flow Framework to Graph Partitioning via Analogy from Statistical Physics
ideas.description: This idea proposes to adapt the solution to analyzing phase transitions from statistical physics to the problem of analyzing various graph partitioning objectives using the expander flow framework. In physics, an order parameter and its fluctuations provide a universal certificate for classifying phase transitions. Analogously, for a graph partitioning objective (like normalized cut or modularity), one can define a corresponding order parameter that evolves during the expander flow process. By studying the scaling behavior and critical points of this parameter and its 'susceptibility' (rate of change), we can develop a new family of flow-based certificates. This would allow us to classify partitioning problems into universality classes based on their spectral and flow properties, creating a unified analytical tool for diverse objectives beyond just edge expansion.

ideas.title: Fast Dual Certification of Expander Flows via Primal-Dual Matrix Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes convex optimization and matrix analysis with the dual certification perspective on expander flows. It develops specialized first-order or interior-point methods that leverage the specific spectral and combinatorial structure of the expander flow dual problem to accelerate the underlying semidefinite program (SDP). The key synergy is that the algorithm's iterates naturally maintain a dual feasible point (a candidate expander flow) while driving a primal matrix variable toward optimality, yielding a fast, duality-gap-certified solution. This enables the practical construction and verification of optimal expanders for applications in coding theory and derandomization at scales previously infeasible for generic SDP solvers.

ideas.title: Dynamic Retreatment: Adaptive Therapy for Prior Non-Responders via Real-Time Resistance Evolution Monitoring
ideas.description: This approach rejects a single, static stratification at the start of retreatment. Instead, it implements a continuous monitoring system for resistance evolution during the retreatment course itself, using frequent liquid biopsies and rapid ex vivo functional drug sensitivity testing on circulating tumor cells or DNA. A clinical decision-support algorithm, informed by these real-time data and known resistance pathways, dynamically recommends treatment modifications—such as switching agents, adjusting doses, or introducing combination therapies—to preemptively counteract the evolving resistance mechanism. The goal is to guide the tumor's evolutionary trajectory towards a more controllable state, effectively turning retreatment into an adaptive, managed process rather than a second static intervention.

ideas.title: Efficient, Constructive Spectral SDP Relaxations for Expander Flow Certification
ideas.description: This research develops a practical certification system for graph expansion by constructing a computationally efficient hierarchy of spectral SDP relaxations, rooted in multi-commodity flow theory. Instead of aiming for an exact, intractable flow, the system produces a tight, polynomial-time computable SDP solution that approximates the optimal multi-commodity flow value. A key component is a deterministic rounding and witness extraction algorithm that transforms the SDP solution into a concrete, easily verifiable combinatorial certificate, such as a cut demonstrating poor expansion or a low-distortion embedding. This approach ensures the certificate is both theoretically grounded and algorithmically tractable for construction and verification.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Energy Landscape Navigation to SDP Optimization
ideas.description: This idea applies analogical reasoning from computational biology's approach to navigating complex energy landscapes in protein folding to the problem of speeding up Semidefinite Programming (SDP). The analogous solution involves using coarse-grained models to find promising 'basins' before applying precise optimization, thus avoiding exhaustive search. Adapted to convex optimization and matrix analysis for SDPs, the research idea is to develop a two-stage hierarchical algorithm. In the first stage, a fast, approximate convex relaxation (like a carefully constructed LP or SOCP) is solved to identify a promising low-dimensional subspace or a neighborhood in the solution space—the 'basin'. In the second stage, the full SDP with its expensive semidefinite constraints is solved, but its search is strategically restricted or warm-started within this identified promising region. This adaptation aims to reduce the effective dimensionality and complexity of the SDP problem, leading to faster convergence and lower computational cost per iteration for the core SDP solver.

ideas.title: Seed-Robust Local Partitioning via Multi-Node Diffusion and Consensus
ideas.description: This research develops a local graph partitioning system that is robust to poor seed selection. Instead of relying on a single seed node, the algorithm performs a limited multi-seed exploration using personalized PageRank or heat kernel diffusion from a small, strategically chosen set of initial points. It then employs a stability analysis and consensus mechanism to identify a partition that is consistent across multiple good seeds or that emerges from the overlap of diffusion regions. This approach guarantees that the output cut is a meaningful, well-connected community surrounding the region of interest, not an artifact of a single unfortunate seed choice.

ideas.title: Finding Largest Well-Separated Subgraphs via Size-Constrained Maximum Conductance
ideas.description: This research formalizes and solves the problem of identifying the largest possible subgraph that remains well-separated from the rest of the network, defined by maximizing conductance subject to a minimum size constraint. It develops algorithms to trace the Pareto frontier between subgraph size and separation quality, revealing structurally distinct regions that are both meaningful in scale and sharply defined. The approach enables the discovery of core-periphery structures, insulated functional modules, or potential attack surfaces in complex networks. This turns the ill-posed maximization into a principled tool for multi-scale graph analysis.

ideas.title: Localized Seismic Tomography for Graph Partitioning
ideas.description: This research adapts the localized approach from seismic tomography to develop local graph partitioning algorithms. In geophysics, full-waveform inversion for entire regions is computationally prohibitive, so researchers developed localized methods that focus imaging on specific subsurface volumes using carefully constructed boundary conditions while treating surrounding regions as known background. Similarly, we propose developing graph partitioning algorithms that only explore and optimize cuts within a localized subgraph around seed nodes, treating the rest of the graph as fixed background with simplified boundary conditions. This approach would create localized versions of spectral methods or flow algorithms that operate within bounded regions, using boundary conditions that approximate global connectivity while dramatically reducing computational requirements compared to global partitioning methods, enabling scalable analysis of massive graphs near specific nodes of interest.

ideas.title: Relaxing Metric Axioms: Incorporating Non-Triangular Constraints from Statistical Physics
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that triangle inequalities are the primary or only valid non-trivial constraints for tightening semidefinite relaxations. By drawing an analogy to statistical physics, specifically spin glass theory, we note that complex systems are solved by moving beyond simple pairwise interaction constraints to incorporate hierarchical and higher-order correlation structures (e.g., the Parisi ultrametric solution). The adapted idea is to investigate analogous 'higher-order' or 'structural' constraints for SDP matrices. This involves formulating and testing new SDP constraints inspired by concepts like overlap distributions and replica symmetry breaking, which could capture more complex relationships among variables than triangle inequalities, potentially leading to tighter and more accurate relaxations for combinatorial optimization problems.

ideas.title: Adapting Fluid Mixing Analysis to Spectral Graph Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from fluid mixing theory to spectral graph theory. In fluid dynamics, the mixing efficiency of a flow is analyzed by observing the decay of high-frequency spatial modes of a passive scalar (like dye), a process quantified using spectral methods like the mix-norm. The analogous problem in graphs is evaluating how quickly a random walk or diffusion process mixes on a graph, which is central to partitioning. The adapted solution treats an initial probability distribution on nodes as the dye blob and the graph diffusion as the mixing flow. By defining a graph-theoretic mix-norm based on the Laplacian's eigenbasis, we can directly link the expansion properties of the graph (expander flows) to spectral gaps and derive new Cheeger-type inequalities that bound mixing times. This creates a novel methodological bridge for analyzing and improving spectral partitioning algorithms through the lens of dynamical mixing.

ideas.description: This approach generalizes expander flow methods to weighted graphs with extreme capacity and demand ranges by first partitioning the graph into logarithmic-scale bands where weights are roughly uniform. It then applies a hierarchical expander decomposition within each band, maintaining strong conductance guarantees locally. A novel inter-band routing scheme coordinates flow between bands using carefully sized buffer edges to prevent congestion, ensuring the overall algorithm runs in polynomial time and achieves provable approximation ratios independent of the input weight range.

ideas.title: Adapting Fluid Dynamics to Hypergraph/Directed Graph Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea extends geometric embedding and expander flow methods to hypergraph or directed sparsest cut by drawing an analogy from fluid dynamics. In fluid dynamics, complex multi-phase flows (like oil-water mixtures) are analyzed by treating the system as a continuum with interacting phases, extending the Navier-Stokes equations with additional terms (like surface tension at interfaces) and solving them with methods that track phase boundaries. Adapted to this research context, we treat a hypergraph or directed graph as a 'multi-phase informational fluid' where hyperedges/directed edges create complex, non-local interactions between node 'particles'. The solution is to develop a continuum flow formulation where these interactions are modeled as generalized pressure or tension forces acting on a geometric embedding of the nodes. The sparsest cut then corresponds to finding a minimal-energy separating surface in this continuum, computed via an adapted expander-like flow that respects the multi-way or asymmetric connectivity inherent to hypergraphs or directed edges.

ideas.title: Maximum Conductance Subgraphs via Ising Model Annealing
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from statistical physics to the graph theory problem of finding maximum conductance subgraphs. In condensed matter physics, the Ising model and simulated annealing are used not only to find minimum energy states but also to locate configurations that maximize certain response functions like specific heat or susceptibility. The analogous solution involves modifying the annealing objective to target these maxima. By mapping a graph to a spin system (nodes as spins, edges as interactions), we can design an annealing process where the cost function is defined to maximize conductance—the ratio of cut edges to the minimum of internal or external volume—rather than minimize cut size. This approach transforms the search for maximum conductance subgraphs into an optimization problem solvable via adapted physical annealing algorithms, potentially revealing novel subgraph structures that act as 'bottlenecks' or regions of maximum boundary flux relative to their size.

ideas.title: Applying Metabolic Pathway Analysis to Strengthen Expander Flow Certification
ideas.description: This idea adapts Elementary Flux Mode (EFM) analysis from systems biology to strengthen expander flow certification. In metabolic engineering, EFM analysis decomposes complex metabolic networks into minimal, biologically meaningful pathways that satisfy steady-state constraints, allowing researchers to certify network capabilities through convex combinations of these elementary modes. By applying this approach to multi-commodity flow theory, we can decompose flow networks into irreducible 'elementary flow modes' - minimal multi-commodity flows satisfying all constraints. Certification of expander flows then reduces to proving that a target flow can be expressed as a convex combination of these elementary modes, providing a more structured, combinatorially rich certificate that leverages the inherent modularity of flow networks while maintaining computational tractability through decomposition methods.

ideas.title: Generalizing Expander Flows via Continuum Mechanics Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of generalizing expander flow algorithms to weighted graphs by drawing an analogy from continuum mechanics. In fluid dynamics, flow through heterogeneous porous media with varying permeability is successfully modeled using Darcy's law with spatially dependent parameters and homogenization techniques. Adapting this approach, we treat the graph as a discrete porous medium where edge capacities correspond to permeability and vertex demands act as sources/sinks. The solution involves developing a continuum approximation using capacity-weighted graph operators, then applying mathematical homogenization to derive effective equations that capture how non-uniform edge capacities influence flow distribution and expansion properties. This could lead to new algorithms that handle arbitrary capacities and demands by solving the homogenized continuum problem and mapping back to discrete solutions.
